9.1.1.intuition: Think of a URL shortener like a coat check at a theater. You hand over your bulky coat (long URL), receive a small numbered ticket (short code), and when you return with the ticket, you get your coat back instantly. The system needs to be fast at both issuing tickets and retrieving coats, and it must never give two people the same ticket number.

exercises.title: Implement Base62 Encoding
exercises.description: Write a Base62 encoder and decoder in JavaScript. The encoder should convert a numeric ID to a string using characters [0-9A-Za-z]. The decoder should convert back to a number. Verify that encodeBase62(decodeBase62(x)) === x for several test values. Calculate how many unique 7-character codes Base62 can produce.
exercises.hint: Use modulo (%) and integer division (Math.floor) in a loop. For decoding, multiply each character's index by 62^position. 62^7 = 3,521,614,606,208.

exercises.title: Build the Redirect Service with Caching
exercises.description: Implement an Express endpoint GET /:shortCode that first checks a Redis cache, falls back to a database lookup, populates the cache on miss, and returns a 302 redirect. Use ioredis and Prisma. Include expiration checking -- expired URLs should return 410 Gone.
exercises.hint: Use redis.get() for cache check, prisma.url.findUnique() for DB lookup, and redis.setex() to cache with TTL. Compare expiresAt with new Date() to check expiration.

exercises.title: Collision-Resistant Short Code Generator
exercises.description: Implement a createShortUrl function that uses MD5 hashing with collision handling. If the generated 7-character code already exists in the database, append a counter to the input and rehash. Write tests that simulate a collision scenario by mocking the database to reject the first attempt.
exercises.hint: Use crypto.createHash('md5').update(url + suffix).digest('base64url').slice(0, 7). Catch Prisma unique constraint errors (code P2002) and retry with an incremented suffix.

exercises.title: Design the Analytics Pipeline
exercises.description: Design and implement an async click-tracking pipeline using BullMQ. When a redirect occurs, enqueue a job with the short code, IP, user agent, and referrer. Create a worker that processes these events and inserts them into a click_events table. Ensure the redirect response is not delayed by analytics processing.
exercises.hint: Use Queue.add() in the redirect handler (fire-and-forget). Create a Worker that listens on the queue and calls prisma.clickEvent.create(). The redirect should call res.redirect() before the analytics job completes.

problems.problem: Short codes are predictable and users can enumerate all URLs
problems.cause: Using sequential auto-increment IDs with Base62 encoding produces sequential short codes (aB3, aB4, aB5...), making it trivial to guess the next URL.
problems.solution: Use a pre-generated random key service, or hash-based generation. If using auto-increment, apply a bijective scrambling function (e.g., multiply by a large prime modulo a larger prime) before Base62 encoding to destroy sequentiality.

problems.problem: Database becomes a bottleneck under heavy redirect traffic
problems.cause: Every redirect performs a database lookup. At 4,000+ requests per second, the database connection pool is exhausted and queries queue up.
problems.solution: Add a Redis caching layer in front of the database. Since URL mappings are immutable (the long URL never changes), they are perfect for aggressive caching with long TTLs. Cache hit rates above 90% are typical.

problems.problem: Click counts are inaccurate under high concurrency
problems.cause: Multiple concurrent requests read the same click_count, increment it, and write back, causing lost updates (classic race condition).
problems.solution: Use atomic database increments (UPDATE urls SET click_count = click_count + 1), or better, decouple click counting into an async pipeline using a message queue and aggregate counts periodically.

further_reading.note: Real-world lessons from scaling a URL shortener to billions of links

9.1.2.intuition: A rate limiter is like a bouncer at a nightclub with a clicker counter. The bouncer counts everyone entering in the last hour. Once the count hits capacity, new arrivals are turned away with a polite 'please come back later.' The bouncer does not care who you are by default, but VIP list members (premium API keys) get a higher personal limit.

exercises.title: Implement Fixed Window Rate Limiter
exercises.description: Build an Express middleware that implements fixed window rate limiting using Redis. It should take configuration for max requests and window size, generate keys based on client IP, set appropriate X-RateLimit-* headers on every response, and return 429 with a Retry-After header when the limit is exceeded. Test it with a loop of rapid requests.
exercises.hint: Use redis.incr() for atomic increment and redis.pexpire() for window expiry. The window key should include Math.floor(Date.now() / windowMs) to create discrete time buckets.

exercises.title: Token Bucket with Lua Script
exercises.description: Implement the token bucket algorithm using a Redis Lua script for atomicity. The bucket should have a configurable capacity and refill rate. Tokens are consumed on each request and replenished based on elapsed time. Write a test that sends a burst of requests, verifies the burst is allowed up to bucket capacity, and then verifies subsequent requests are throttled until tokens refill.
exercises.hint: Store tokens and last_refill_time in a Redis hash. In the Lua script, calculate elapsed time, add refilled tokens (capped at bucket_size), then try to consume one token. Use redis.eval() to run the script.

exercises.title: Multi-Tier Rate Limiting
exercises.description: Create a rate limiting system with three tiers: global per-IP limit (1000/min), per-route limit (100/min for /api/*, 10/15min for /auth/login), and per-user limit (50/min for authenticated users). Load rules from a database table and apply them as Express middleware. Ensure the most restrictive limit wins.
exercises.hint: Create a middleware factory that accepts an array of rules. For each request, evaluate all matching rules and reject if any one is exceeded. Use route pattern matching (e.g., minimatch or path-to-regexp) to match rules to routes.

problems.problem: Rate limiter allows double the configured limit at window boundaries
problems.cause: The fixed window counter algorithm resets at exact window boundaries. A client can send max requests at the end of one window and max more at the start of the next, resulting in 2x the limit in a short period.
problems.solution: Switch to the sliding window counter algorithm, which weights the previous window's count by how much of it overlaps with the current sliding window. This smooths out the boundary burst problem.

problems.problem: Rate limiter blocks all traffic when Redis goes down
problems.cause: The middleware throws an unhandled exception when the Redis connection fails, causing Express to return 500 errors for every request.
problems.solution: Wrap Redis calls in try/catch and implement fail-open behavior: if Redis is unreachable, allow the request through. Log the error for alerting. Optionally, fall back to an in-memory counter as a degraded mode.

problems.problem: Race condition allows more requests than the limit under high concurrency
problems.cause: The check-then-increment pattern (read count, compare to limit, then increment) is not atomic. Multiple concurrent requests can read the same count before any of them increment it.
problems.solution: Use Redis Lua scripts to make the check-and-increment operation atomic. Redis executes Lua scripts as a single, uninterruptible operation, eliminating race conditions.

9.1.3.intuition: A notification service works like a post office. Letters (notifications) arrive, get sorted by destination type (email, SMS, push -- like domestic, international, express), placed into separate processing lines, and handed off to delivery trucks (providers like SendGrid, Twilio). If one truck breaks down, the other routes keep flowing. And just like real mail, you can track whether it was delivered, opened, or returned to sender.

exercises.title: Build a Multi-Channel Notification Worker
exercises.description: Implement a BullMQ worker that processes notification jobs. Each job specifies a user_id, template_id, and channels array. The worker should render the template, send to each channel using mock provider functions, record delivery status in the database, and handle partial failures (some channels succeed, others fail). Use Prisma for database operations.
exercises.hint: Use Promise.allSettled() to send to all channels in parallel without short-circuiting on failure. Check each result's status to determine if it was 'fulfilled' or 'rejected' and update delivery records accordingly.

exercises.title: Implement User Preference Resolution
exercises.description: Write a resolveChannels() function that takes requested channels, user preferences, and notification category as input and returns the final list of channels to send on. It must filter out disabled channels, respect category opt-outs, check quiet hours (skip non-critical during quiet hours), and handle the 'daily_digest' email frequency preference.
exercises.hint: Parse quiet hours using the user's timezone (use luxon or date-fns-tz). For daily_digest, queue the email notification instead of sending immediately. Return an object with { immediate: ['push'], deferred: ['email'] }.

exercises.title: Notification Deduplication System
exercises.description: Build an idempotency layer that prevents duplicate notifications. Use Redis SET with NX (set-if-not-exists) to track idempotency keys. Write tests that verify: (1) the first request succeeds, (2) a retry with the same key returns the cached response, (3) keys expire after 24 hours.
exercises.hint: Use redis.set(key, value, 'EX', 86400, 'NX') which atomically sets the key only if it does not exist. If the return value is null, the key already existed (duplicate). Store the original response JSON as the value.

exercises.title: Provider Circuit Breaker
exercises.description: Implement a circuit breaker pattern for notification providers. When a provider (e.g., SendGrid) fails 5 times in 60 seconds, the circuit opens and automatically routes to a fallback provider (e.g., SES). After 30 seconds, the circuit enters half-open state and lets one test request through. Track circuit state in Redis for distributed correctness.
exercises.hint: Store failure counts and circuit state in Redis with TTLs. Use three states: CLOSED (normal), OPEN (all requests go to fallback), HALF_OPEN (one test request allowed). Use redis.incr() with pexpire for failure counting.

problems.problem: Users receive duplicate notifications for the same event
problems.cause: Retries from the message queue (due to transient failures or worker crashes) cause the same notification to be processed multiple times, and there is no deduplication check.
problems.solution: Implement idempotency using a unique key per notification event (e.g., 'order_shipped:order_12345'). Before processing, check if the key exists in Redis. Use SET NX to atomically claim the key. Also check the notification_deliveries table for existing records.

problems.problem: SMS provider outage causes push notifications to back up
problems.cause: All notification channels share a single queue. When SMS workers slow down or fail, the queue grows and all channels are affected.
problems.solution: Use separate queues per channel (notifications:push, notifications:email, notifications:sms, notifications:in_app). This provides failure isolation -- an SMS outage has zero impact on push notification delivery.

problems.problem: Users complain about receiving notifications at 3 AM
problems.cause: The notification service does not account for user timezones when evaluating quiet hours. Quiet hours of 22:00-08:00 are evaluated in UTC instead of the user's local time.
problems.solution: Store the user's timezone in preferences. When checking quiet hours, convert the current UTC time to the user's local time before comparison. Use a library like luxon: DateTime.now().setZone(userTimezone).hour to get the local hour.

9.1.4.intuition: A chat system is like a mail room in a massive apartment building. Each resident (user) has a mailbox (WebSocket connection) on a specific floor (server). When someone sends a letter, the mail room (message router) looks up which floor the recipient lives on and delivers it to the right mailbox. If the resident is not home (offline), the letter is held in a safe (pending queue) until they return, and a text message (push notification) is sent to let them know mail is waiting.

exercises.title: Build a WebSocket Chat Server
exercises.description: Implement a basic chat server using the ws library in Node.js. Support user authentication on connection (via query parameter token), maintain an in-memory connection map (userId -> WebSocket), and implement send_message that looks up the recipient's WebSocket and delivers the message. Handle connection cleanup on disconnect. Test with two browser tabs.
exercises.hint: Use wss.on('connection', (ws, req) => { ... }) to handle new connections. Parse the user token from req.url. Store connections in a Map. On message, JSON.parse the payload and look up the recipient in the Map.

exercises.title: Add Offline Message Queue
exercises.description: Extend your chat server to handle offline users. When a message is sent to a user who is not connected, store it in a Redis list. When that user reconnects, deliver all pending messages in order and clear the list. Write a test that sends messages while a user is disconnected, reconnects them, and verifies all messages are delivered in order.
exercises.hint: Use redis.lpush('pending:userId', message) for offline storage and redis.lrange + redis.del on reconnection. Reverse the list since lpush adds to the head.

exercises.title: Implement Read Receipts and Delivery Status
exercises.description: Add three-stage message status tracking: sent (server received), delivered (recipient's device received), read (recipient opened conversation). When a status update occurs, notify the original sender via WebSocket. Store the latest status in the database. Handle the case where the sender is offline when the status update arrives.
exercises.hint: Send 'message_ack' to sender when message is saved (sent). Send 'message_status: delivered' when recipient's WebSocket receives it. Send 'message_status: read' when recipient sends a read_receipt event. Track status changes in a delivery_status column.

problems.problem: Messages arrive out of order in group chats
problems.cause: Messages from different senders are processed by different WebSocket servers and Kafka consumers, leading to non-deterministic delivery order.
problems.solution: Use conversation_id as the Kafka partition key so all messages in a conversation go to the same partition, which guarantees ordering. On the client, sort messages by server-assigned timestamps. Use TIMEUUID or Snowflake IDs that embed timestamps for natural ordering.

problems.problem: WebSocket server runs out of memory with too many connections
problems.cause: Each WebSocket connection consumes memory for the socket buffer and associated state. A single Node.js process trying to handle 200K+ connections exhausts available RAM.
problems.solution: Limit connections per server to 50K-100K. Scale horizontally with more WebSocket server instances behind a load balancer. Use sticky sessions so a user's reconnection attempts go to the same server. Monitor memory per connection and tune buffer sizes.

problems.problem: Presence status shows users as online when they have actually crashed
problems.cause: If a client crashes without cleanly closing the WebSocket, the server may not immediately detect the disconnect. TCP keep-alive timeouts can be minutes long.
problems.solution: Implement application-level heartbeats (ping/pong every 30 seconds). Use Redis key expiry (TTL of 60 seconds) for presence. If a heartbeat is missed, the key expires and the user appears offline. Also configure WebSocket ping intervals on the server side.

9.1.5.intuition: A news feed is like a personalized newspaper. For regular columnists (normal users), their articles are pre-printed and bundled into each subscriber's paper at the printing press (fan-out on write). For headline news from major figures (celebrities), the articles are pulled from the wire service and inserted into your paper right when you pick it up (fan-out on read). The editor (ranking algorithm) decides the front-page order based on what you find most interesting.

exercises.title: Implement Fan-Out on Write
exercises.description: Build a BullMQ worker that processes new posts and pushes them to follower feed caches in Redis. Use Redis sorted sets with timestamps as scores. Implement batched processing (1000 followers per batch) to avoid blocking Redis. Test with a user who has 5000 followers and verify all feeds are updated.
exercises.hint: Use redis.pipeline() to batch ZADD commands. For each batch of 1000 followers, create a pipeline that does zadd('feed:followerId', timestamp, postId) for each follower. Use ZREMRANGEBYRANK to trim feeds to the latest 1000 posts.

exercises.title: Build the Feed Retrieval Endpoint
exercises.description: Implement GET /api/v1/feed with cursor-based pagination. Read post IDs from the user's Redis sorted set, fetch post details from the database, enrich with has_liked status, and return with a cursor for the next page. Handle the cold-start case where the feed cache is empty by building it on the fly from the database.
exercises.hint: Use ZREVRANGEBYSCORE with the cursor score as the max for pagination. For cold start, query posts from followed users in the last 7 days. Encode the cursor as base64({ lastScore: timestamp }). Use prisma.findMany with an IN clause for post IDs.

exercises.title: Implement Hybrid Fan-Out
exercises.description: Modify the post creation flow to use hybrid fan-out. When a regular user (< 10K followers) posts, push to all followers. When a celebrity posts, do not fan out -- instead, modify the feed read path to fetch the celebrity's recent posts and merge them with the cached feed. Test both paths.
exercises.hint: On post creation, check followerCount. On feed read, maintain a list of followed celebrities (store in Redis set). For each celebrity, fetch their last 20 posts from the posts table and merge into the feed results by timestamp before pagination.

exercises.title: Add a Simple Ranking Function
exercises.description: Replace chronological ordering with a ranking function. Score each post based on: recency (exponential decay), engagement (likes + 2*comments + 3*shares, normalized), and author affinity (how often the user has liked the author's posts). Re-sort the feed by ranking score instead of timestamp. Compare the ranked feed to the chronological feed.
exercises.hint: Calculate recencyScore = Math.exp(-0.05 * hoursAgo). For affinity, count likes by the user on the author's posts in the last 30 days, normalize to 0-1. Combine with weights: 0.4*recency + 0.3*engagement + 0.3*affinity. Sort descending by score.

problems.problem: Celebrity posts take minutes to appear in followers' feeds
problems.cause: Fan-out on write for a user with 5M followers requires 5M Redis write operations. Even at 100K ops/second, this takes 50 seconds, during which the fan-out queue is backed up.
problems.solution: Switch to the hybrid model: do not fan-out for users above a follower threshold (e.g., 10K). Instead, fetch their recent posts at feed-read time and merge with the pre-computed feed. This eliminates the write amplification problem for celebrities.

problems.problem: Users see duplicate posts when scrolling through their feed
problems.cause: Using offset-based pagination (page=1, page=2), and new posts are inserted between page loads, shifting items down and causing the same post to appear on both pages.
problems.solution: Switch to cursor-based pagination. Use the last post's timestamp (or ranking score) as the cursor. The next page fetches posts with score less than the cursor, which is stable regardless of new insertions.

problems.problem: Feed cache misses are slow, causing timeouts for users who have not opened the app in days
problems.cause: When a user's feed cache has expired (TTL exceeded) or was never populated, the system must build the entire feed from scratch by querying posts from all followed users -- a slow, expensive operation.
problems.solution: Implement proactive cache warming: when a user opens the app, if the cache is older than 1 hour, start warming in the background. Use a 'stale-while-revalidate' pattern where the stale cache is served immediately while a fresh one is being built. Also, schedule periodic cache warming for frequently active users.

9.2.1.intuition: Think of Dropbox like a post office with lockers. When you upload a file, the clerk (API) does not personally store the package -- they give you a locker number (presigned URL) and you put it in the locker (S3) yourself. To sync, each device keeps a receipt number (cursor). They ask: 'What happened after receipt #4527?' The clerk replies with a list of new packages, moves, and deletions. Chunking is like sending a large parcel in multiple boxes -- if one gets lost in transit, you only resend that box.

exercises.title: Build a Chunked Upload Service
exercises.description: Implement a Node.js service that accepts a file, splits it into 1 MB chunks, computes SHA-256 for each chunk, and uploads them to a local directory. Implement deduplication: if a chunk with the same hash exists, skip the write. Build a reassembly endpoint that reconstructs the original file from chunks.
exercises.hint: Use `node:crypto` for hashing and `node:fs/promises` for file I/O. Store chunk metadata in a Map or SQLite. Use `createReadStream` with `highWaterMark` to read in chunks.

exercises.title: Implement Delta Sync
exercises.description: Build a sync service with a PostgreSQL-backed journal. Clients POST file changes and GET deltas using a cursor. Write a test that simulates two clients syncing through the server, verifying they converge to the same state.
exercises.hint: Use a BIGSERIAL column as the cursor. Return changes WHERE id > cursor ORDER BY id LIMIT 100. The client stores the last received id.

exercises.title: Presigned URL Flow
exercises.description: Using the AWS SDK (@aws-sdk/s3-request-presigner), implement the full presigned URL upload flow: (1) client requests an upload URL, (2) client uploads directly to S3, (3) client notifies your server of completion, (4) server verifies the upload exists in S3 and records metadata.
exercises.hint: Use `getSignedUrl` with `PutObjectCommand`. Set URL expiry to 15 minutes. Use `HeadObjectCommand` to verify the upload on completion.

problems.problem: Large file uploads fail intermittently on slow connections
problems.cause: Single-request uploads time out or consume too much memory when buffering the entire file.
problems.solution: Implement chunked uploads. Split files into 4 MB chunks, upload each independently with retry logic. Use presigned URLs so clients upload directly to object storage. Track chunk completion server-side and reassemble only on final confirmation.

problems.problem: Users see stale file listings after editing on another device
problems.cause: The sync mechanism relies on polling with a long interval, or the notification channel (WebSocket) has silently disconnected.
problems.solution: Use WebSocket push notifications with heartbeat pings to detect disconnections. Implement automatic reconnection with exponential backoff. On reconnect, immediately fetch the delta using the last known cursor.

problems.problem: Storage costs are growing faster than user growth
problems.cause: No deduplication -- multiple users storing the same files (e.g., common documents, OS files) results in redundant storage.
problems.solution: Implement content-addressable storage at the chunk level. Use SHA-256 of chunk content as the storage key. Before writing a chunk, check if it already exists. Maintain a reference count and only delete chunks when the count reaches zero.

9.2.2.intuition: Think of YouTube like a TV broadcast network. When a creator uploads a raw tape, it goes to the editing room (transcoding pipeline) where copies are made for different screen sizes. These copies are shipped to local TV stations (CDN edges) around the world. When a viewer tunes in, they get the feed from their nearest station, and the station automatically adjusts the picture quality based on the viewer's signal strength (adaptive bitrate).

exercises.title: Build a Video Transcoding Worker
exercises.description: Create a BullMQ worker that takes a video file path, transcodes it to 360p and 720p using FFmpeg, splits it into HLS segments, generates a master playlist, and stores everything in a local directory. Verify playback with an HLS player.
exercises.hint: Install ffmpeg locally. Use `child_process.execFile` to run ffmpeg commands. The HLS master playlist is a text file referencing each resolution's playlist.m3u8.

exercises.title: Implement Buffered View Counting
exercises.description: Build a system where view events are recorded in Redis using INCR and periodically flushed to PostgreSQL. Implement the flush as a scheduled BullMQ job that runs every 30 seconds. Verify that total counts in PostgreSQL match Redis after multiple flush cycles.
exercises.hint: Use Redis GETSET to atomically read and reset the counter during flush. Use `ioredis.pipeline()` to batch reads for multiple videos.

exercises.title: Adaptive Bitrate Simulation
exercises.description: Write a Node.js simulation of an adaptive bitrate algorithm. Given an array of available bitrates and a fluctuating bandwidth (random values between 1-10 Mbps), implement the logic that selects the highest quality that fits within 80% of available bandwidth. Log quality switches over a 5-minute simulated viewing session.
exercises.hint: Buffer-based ABR: if buffer > 30s, try upgrading quality; if buffer < 10s, downgrade. Bandwidth-based: use exponential moving average of recent download speeds.

problems.problem: Video playback frequently buffers for users in certain regions
problems.cause: CDN cache miss rate is high in those regions, causing viewers to fetch segments from the origin server with high latency.
problems.solution: Analyze CDN cache hit rates by region. Pre-warm popular content at edge locations. Add more CDN points of presence in underserved regions. Consider a multi-CDN strategy where you route to the CDN with best performance per region.

problems.problem: Transcoding queue grows unboundedly during peak upload hours
problems.cause: Fixed number of transcoding workers cannot keep up with upload spikes. Some videos are very long (hours) and block workers.
problems.solution: Use auto-scaling worker pools (ECS/Kubernetes HPA). Prioritize shorter videos and popular creators. Split long videos into segments for parallel transcoding. Set a maximum queue depth with back-pressure that temporarily rejects new uploads.

problems.problem: View count shows different numbers on the video page vs the creator dashboard
problems.cause: Buffered view counting creates a delay between Redis (real-time) and PostgreSQL (flushed every 30s). Dashboard reads from PostgreSQL while the video page reads from Redis.
problems.solution: Read from Redis for the video page (real-time approximate count). Read from PostgreSQL for analytics dashboards (accurate, slightly delayed). Document the expected delay. Alternatively, merge both: PostgreSQL count + Redis unflushed delta.

9.2.3.intuition: Think of a key-value store as a network of filing cabinets (nodes), each responsible for a range of alphabetical folders (partitions). When you store a document, a receptionist (coordinator) figures out which cabinets it belongs in (consistent hashing), makes three copies (replication factor), and files them in three different cabinets. When you retrieve it, the receptionist asks two cabinets and takes the most recent copy (quorum read). If a cabinet is locked (node down), someone else holds the document temporarily and files it later (hinted handoff).

exercises.title: Build an In-Memory Key-Value Store
exercises.description: Implement a single-node key-value store in Node.js with get, put, delete, and TTL support. Use a Map for storage and a separate data structure for TTL expiration. Expose it via an Express REST API. Benchmark it with autocannon to measure throughput.
exercises.hint: For TTL, use a min-heap (priority queue) sorted by expiration time. Run a setInterval that pops expired keys. Alternatively, check TTL lazily on read.

exercises.title: Implement Consistent Hashing with Virtual Nodes
exercises.description: Build a consistent hash ring in Node.js. Add 5 physical nodes with 100 virtual nodes each. Hash 10,000 random keys and verify distribution is within 20% of perfect balance. Then remove one node and verify that only ~20% of keys are remapped.
exercises.hint: Use MD5 for hashing. Count keys per physical node before and after removal. Calculate standard deviation to measure balance.

exercises.title: Simulate Quorum Reads and Writes
exercises.description: Create a simulation of 3 replica nodes with configurable network delay and failure probability. Implement quorum writes (W=2) and reads (R=2). Inject a node failure and verify that the system still operates correctly. Test that W+R > N guarantees reading the latest write.
exercises.hint: Simulate nodes as objects with a `write(key, value)` and `read(key)` method that randomly delay or throw. Use Promise.allSettled to collect results.

exercises.title: Build a Write-Ahead Log
exercises.description: Implement a simple WAL that appends key-value operations to a file. On startup, replay the WAL to reconstruct the in-memory state. Benchmark the performance difference between fsync on every write vs. batched fsync every 100ms.
exercises.hint: Use `fs.appendFile` or `fs.createWriteStream` in append mode. For replay, read line by line with readline. Use `fs.fdatasync` for fsync.

problems.problem: Hot keys causing uneven load across nodes
problems.cause: Certain keys (e.g., a viral post's like counter) receive millions of operations per second, overloading the nodes responsible for that key's partition.
problems.solution: Implement key splitting: append a random suffix (e.g., `key:0` through `key:9`) to spread the hot key across multiple nodes. On read, query all shards and aggregate. Alternatively, use a local cache on coordinator nodes for read-heavy hot keys.

problems.problem: Data inconsistency after network partition heals
problems.cause: During a network partition, both sides accepted writes for the same keys. When the partition heals, replicas have conflicting values.
problems.solution: Use vector clocks to detect true conflicts. For last-write-wins, ensure all nodes use synchronized clocks (NTP with bounded drift). Run anti-entropy repair using Merkle tree comparison to identify and resolve divergent keys.

problems.problem: Read latency increasing as data grows
problems.cause: Too many SSTables on disk. Each read must check multiple SSTables (read amplification). Bloom filters help but do not eliminate all unnecessary reads.
problems.solution: Implement tiered or leveled compaction to merge SSTables. Leveled compaction (LevelDB-style) bounds read amplification to O(log N). Monitor compaction throughput and ensure it keeps pace with write throughput.

further_reading.title: Designing Data-Intensive Applications - Chapters 5 & 6
further_reading.note: Replication and Partitioning chapters are essential reading for this topic

further_reading.title: Amazon DynamoDB Paper
further_reading.note: The original Dynamo paper introducing consistent hashing, quorum, vector clocks, and anti-entropy

further_reading.title: Redis Internals Documentation
further_reading.note: How Redis implements its data structures and persistence

further_reading.title: LSM Tree Explained
further_reading.note: Original LSM-tree paper by O'Neil et al.

9.2.4.intuition: A search engine is like a massive library with a card catalog. The web crawler is a team of librarians constantly visiting bookstores (websites), bringing back books (pages), and noting what each book is about. The indexer builds the card catalog (inverted index) -- under each subject, it lists which books mention it. When a patron (user) asks a question, the librarian does not read every book. Instead, they look up the subject in the card catalog, find matching books, and rank them by quality (PageRank) and relevance (TF-IDF). The most popular questions are posted on a bulletin board (cache) so the librarian does not even need to check the catalog.

exercises.title: Build a Mini Search Engine
exercises.description: Implement an in-memory inverted index in Node.js. Index 100 text documents (use Wikipedia excerpts or Lorem Ipsum). Implement TF-IDF scoring. Expose a search API that returns the top 10 results with scores and snippets highlighting matching terms.
exercises.hint: Tokenize by splitting on whitespace, lowercasing, and removing stop words. For snippets, find the sentence containing the most query terms.

exercises.title: Implement PageRank
exercises.description: Build a simple web graph with 20 nodes and random edges. Implement the PageRank algorithm and verify that well-connected hub nodes get higher scores. Combine PageRank with TF-IDF to create a composite ranking score.
exercises.hint: Use an adjacency list for the graph. Run 20-50 iterations of PageRank until convergence (delta < 0.0001). Normalize TF-IDF and PageRank scores before combining.

exercises.title: Query Fan-Out Simulation
exercises.description: Simulate a query coordinator that fans out to 10 index shards (each a separate in-memory inverted index). Each shard returns its top 5 results. Implement a merge function that re-ranks all 50 results and returns the global top 10. Measure fan-out latency.
exercises.hint: Create 10 InvertedIndex instances, each with a different subset of documents. Use Promise.all to query them in parallel. The merge function sorts by score descending.

problems.problem: Search latency spikes during index updates
problems.cause: Updating the inverted index in-place while serving queries causes lock contention and cache invalidation.
problems.solution: Use an immutable segment-based approach. Build new index segments offline, then atomically swap them into the serving path. Old segments are garbage collected after all in-flight queries complete. This is how Lucene/Elasticsearch work.

problems.problem: Stale results for rapidly changing pages (e.g., news sites)
problems.cause: The batch crawl and index pipeline has a multi-hour delay between crawling a page and it appearing in search results.
problems.solution: Implement a real-time indexing pipeline: high-priority URLs (news sites, social media) are crawled every few minutes via a streaming pipeline (Kafka). Updates are applied to a small real-time index that is merged with the main index at query time.

problems.problem: Crawler overloading target websites
problems.cause: No rate limiting per domain. The crawler sends hundreds of concurrent requests to the same domain.
problems.solution: Implement per-domain rate limiting in the URL frontier. Respect robots.txt crawl-delay directives. Use a domain-based queue where each domain has a minimum interval between requests (e.g., 1 request per second per domain).

further_reading.title: The Anatomy of a Large-Scale Search Engine (Google, 1998)
further_reading.note: The original Google paper by Brin and Page

further_reading.title: Elasticsearch: The Definitive Guide
further_reading.note: Understand how a production-grade inverted index works

further_reading.title: Introduction to Information Retrieval
further_reading.note: Free online textbook covering TF-IDF, inverted indexes, and ranking algorithms

further_reading.title: How Google Search Works
further_reading.note: Google's own explanation of crawling, indexing, and ranking

9.2.5.intuition: A distributed cache is like a network of speed-dial phone books. Each phone book (cache node) holds a subset of frequently called numbers (cached values). When you need a number, your personal assistant (client library) knows exactly which phone book to check (consistent hashing). If the number is there, you get it instantly. If not, you look it up in the full directory (database) and write it in the phone book for next time. When a phone book is full, the least recently looked-up number is erased (LRU eviction). If a phone book is lost (node failure), you do not panic -- you can always look numbers up in the full directory. The key insight is that losing a phone book is annoying but not catastrophic, because the directory (database) is the source of truth.

exercises.title: Implement an LRU Cache
exercises.description: Build an LRU cache in Node.js using a Map and a doubly linked list. Support get(key), set(key, value, ttl), and delete(key). All operations must be O(1). Add a stats endpoint that reports hit rate, miss rate, and eviction count. Benchmark with 1M operations.
exercises.hint: Use a Map for O(1) key lookup. The doubly linked list maintains access order. On get, move the node to the head. On set, add to head and evict from tail if full.

exercises.title: Build a Distributed Cache Client
exercises.description: Create a cache client that distributes keys across 3 local cache server instances using consistent hashing. Implement get, set, delete, and multi-get. Test that adding a 4th node only remaps approximately 25% of keys.
exercises.hint: Run 3 Express servers on different ports. Use the ConsistentHashRing class from the lesson. For multi-get, group keys by node and send parallel requests.

exercises.title: Implement Cache Stampede Protection
exercises.description: Build a cache-aside pattern with stampede protection using Redis locks. Simulate a thundering herd by firing 100 concurrent requests for the same cache key after it expires. Compare database hit count with and without stampede protection.
exercises.hint: Use Redis SET with NX and EX flags for the lock. Waiting requests should retry after a short delay. Count DB calls with a counter to verify protection works.

exercises.title: Eviction Policy Comparison
exercises.description: Implement LRU, LFU, and Random eviction policies. Generate a workload with Zipfian distribution (some keys much more popular than others). Measure and compare hit rates across all three policies for the same cache size.
exercises.hint: For Zipfian distribution, use the formula: probability of accessing item i is proportional to 1/i^s where s is the skew parameter. Use s=1.0 for typical web workloads.

problems.problem: Cache hit rate drops significantly after adding a new node
problems.cause: Using modulo-based hashing (key hash % N). When N changes from 3 to 4 nodes, up to 75% of keys are remapped to different nodes, causing mass cache misses.
problems.solution: Switch to consistent hashing with virtual nodes. When a node is added to a ring with N nodes, only approximately 1/N of keys are remapped. Use 100+ virtual nodes per physical node for even distribution.

problems.problem: Thundering herd on database after cache node failure
problems.cause: All keys assigned to the failed node result in cache misses. All application servers simultaneously query the database for those keys.
problems.solution: Implement a gutter pool: a small set of reserve cache nodes. When a primary cache node is unreachable, route its keys to the gutter pool instead of going to the database. Use short TTLs in the gutter pool. Also consider warming the replacement node from a database snapshot.

problems.problem: Memory usage on cache nodes is uneven -- some nodes are 95% full while others are 50%
problems.cause: Poor distribution of keys across the hash ring, or some keys have significantly larger values than others.
problems.solution: Increase virtual nodes per physical node (150+) for more even distribution. Monitor per-node memory and key counts. Consider value-size-aware routing: route large values to nodes with more available memory. Alternatively, use slab rebalancing to reclaim memory from underused slab classes.

further_reading.title: Scaling Memcache at Facebook
further_reading.note: The seminal paper on scaling Memcached to handle billions of requests -- introduces gutter pools, leases, and regional replication

further_reading.title: Memcached Internals
further_reading.note: Official wiki explaining slab allocation, LRU, and the text/binary protocol

further_reading.title: Redis Cluster Specification
further_reading.note: How Redis implements distributed caching with hash slots and replication

further_reading.title: A Hitchhiker's Guide to Caching Patterns
further_reading.note: Comprehensive overview of cache-aside, read-through, write-through, and write-behind patterns

9.3.1.intuition: Think of a physical department store. The product catalog is the store layout and shelf labels. The shopping cart is literally a cart you push around. Checkout is the cash register. Inventory management is the warehouse in back -- you cannot sell what is not on the shelf or in the back room. The key insight is that browsing (reads) and buying (writes) have very different consistency needs: it is fine if the website shows a product for a few seconds after it sells out, but you must never charge someone for an item you cannot deliver.

exercises.title: Build a Cart Service with Redis
exercises.description: Implement a full shopping cart API using Express and ioredis. Support add, remove, update quantity, and get cart. Use Redis hashes to store cart items. Include cart expiration (7 days) and stock validation on add.
exercises.hint: Use `redis.hset(cartKey, productId, JSON.stringify(item))` for individual items. Use `redis.hgetall(cartKey)` to fetch the full cart. Validate stock by checking inventory before adding.

exercises.title: Inventory Reservation System
exercises.description: Build an inventory service that uses pessimistic locking (SELECT FOR UPDATE) to prevent overselling. Write a test that simulates 100 concurrent purchase attempts for a product with only 10 items in stock. Verify exactly 10 succeed.
exercises.hint: Use Prisma $transaction with raw queries for SELECT FOR UPDATE. Use Promise.all to simulate concurrent requests. Assert that inventory never goes negative.

exercises.title: Product Search with Elasticsearch
exercises.description: Set up Elasticsearch with Docker, index 1000 sample products, and build a search API that supports full-text search, category filtering, price range facets, and sorting by relevance or price.
exercises.hint: Use the @elastic/elasticsearch client. Define mappings with keyword fields for exact match and text fields for full-text search. Use aggregations for facets.

problems.problem: Products showing as available but failing at checkout (overselling)
problems.cause: The product listing page uses cached data showing available stock, but by the time the user checks out, the actual inventory has been depleted by other concurrent purchases. No locking mechanism prevents the race condition.
problems.solution: Use pessimistic locking (SELECT FOR UPDATE) or optimistic locking (version column) during the checkout transaction. Keep real-time inventory counts in Redis with atomic DECR operations. Accept that catalog pages may briefly show stale stock -- validate at checkout.
problems.symptoms: Customer complaints about orders being cancelled after payment. Order fulfillment team reports negative inventory counts in the database.

problems.problem: Search results are stale or missing newly added products
problems.cause: Elasticsearch index is not synchronized with the product database. If using periodic batch reindexing, there can be significant lag. If using CDC (Change Data Capture), the pipeline may have failed silently.
problems.solution: Implement CDC with Kafka/Debezium for near-real-time sync. Add a monitoring dashboard for index lag. Provide a manual reindex endpoint for emergencies. Consider dual-write as fallback (write to both DB and ES, accept occasional inconsistency).

problems.problem: Site crashes during flash sales with 10x traffic spike
problems.cause: The system was designed for normal traffic patterns. Flash sales cause thundering herd on the database, cache stampede when cached items expire simultaneously, and API gateway rate limits being hit.
problems.solution: Pre-warm caches before the sale. Use Redis atomic operations for inventory instead of database writes. Implement a virtual queue so users wait in line rather than overwhelming the system. Auto-scale application servers based on queue depth. Use circuit breakers on downstream services.

further_reading.title: Designing Data-Intensive Applications (Chapter 7: Transactions)
further_reading.note: Essential reading on transaction isolation levels and concurrency control -- directly applicable to inventory management

further_reading.title: How Amazon Handles Flash Sales
further_reading.note: AWS architecture blog covers patterns for handling traffic spikes

further_reading.title: Elasticsearch Official Node.js Client
further_reading.note: Complete guide to using Elasticsearch from Node.js

further_reading.title: Building Microservices by Sam Newman
further_reading.note: Covers service decomposition strategies relevant to breaking an e-commerce monolith into services

9.3.2.intuition: Think of a payment system like a very meticulous accountant. Every time money moves, the accountant writes two entries in an ink-only ledger: one account goes up, another goes down. If they make a mistake, they do not erase -- they write a new correcting entry. The idempotency key is like a receipt number: if you show the same receipt twice, the accountant says 'already processed' instead of recording it again. This is exactly how banks have worked for centuries -- the digital version just needs to handle it at internet speed.

exercises.title: Build an Idempotency Layer
exercises.description: Implement an Express middleware that handles idempotency keys. Store results in Redis with a 24-hour TTL. Handle concurrent requests with the same key using Redis locks (SET NX). Write tests that verify: same key returns cached result, different keys process independently, concurrent same-key requests do not duplicate.
exercises.hint: Use `redis.set(key, value, 'EX', ttl, 'NX')` for the lock. Use a separate key for the cached response. Wrap `res.json` to capture the response before sending.

exercises.title: Implement a Double-Entry Ledger
exercises.description: Build a ledger service with Prisma that records financial transactions as paired debit/credit entries. Implement balance calculation, integrity verification (total debits = total credits), and a transaction history endpoint. Test with payment and refund scenarios.
exercises.hint: Use a database transaction to ensure both entries are created atomically. Add a CHECK constraint or trigger to prevent UPDATEs on the ledger table. Calculate balances by summing credits minus debits per account.

exercises.title: Webhook Delivery System
exercises.description: Build a webhook delivery system using BullMQ. Implement HMAC-SHA256 signature generation, exponential backoff retries (1min, 5min, 30min, 2hr), and a dead letter queue for permanently failed deliveries. Include a dashboard endpoint showing delivery status.
exercises.hint: Use `crypto.createHmac('sha256', secret)` for signing. Configure BullMQ backoff with `{ type: 'exponential', delay: 60000 }`. Use `job.attemptsMade` to track retry count.

problems.problem: Customers are being double-charged for the same purchase
problems.cause: Network timeout during payment processing causes the client to retry. Without idempotency, the retry creates a second charge. The card network processes both authorizations as separate transactions.
problems.solution: Require idempotency keys on all payment creation endpoints. Before processing, check if the key exists in Redis. Use a distributed lock to prevent concurrent processing of the same key. Return the cached result for duplicate requests.
problems.symptoms: Customer support reports of duplicate charges. Bank statements show two identical charges seconds apart. Transaction logs show two payment records with different IDs but same amount and card.

problems.problem: Ledger balance does not match actual bank account balance
problems.cause: A payment was recorded in the ledger but the bank transfer failed, or a bank transfer succeeded but the ledger entry was not created due to application crash between the two operations.
problems.solution: Use the Saga pattern: record the ledger entry and bank transfer request in the same database transaction. Process the bank transfer asynchronously. Reconcile daily by comparing ledger totals with bank statements. Flag discrepancies for manual review.

problems.problem: Webhook deliveries are backing up, causing merchants to miss payment notifications
problems.cause: One merchant's webhook endpoint is slow or down, and the retry mechanism is consuming all worker capacity. Other merchants' webhooks are delayed because they share the same queue.
problems.solution: Use separate queues or rate limits per merchant. Implement circuit breakers: after N consecutive failures, stop attempting delivery to that merchant and alert them. Use priority queues so first-attempt deliveries are not blocked by retries.

further_reading.title: Stripe's API Design and Idempotency
further_reading.note: Stripe's official documentation on idempotency -- the gold standard for payment API design

further_reading.title: Designing Data-Intensive Applications (Chapter 9: Consistency and Consensus)
further_reading.note: Covers exactly-once semantics and distributed transactions relevant to payment processing

further_reading.title: PCI DSS Compliance Guide
further_reading.note: Required reading for anyone building systems that handle credit card data

further_reading.title: Martin Fowler on Event Sourcing
further_reading.note: Event sourcing patterns directly apply to immutable financial ledgers

9.3.3.intuition: Think of a hotel booking system like a physical reservation book at a front desk. The book has one page per room type per night. When someone books, the receptionist crosses off one room on each relevant page. If the last room is crossed off, that room type is full for that night. Two receptionists cannot cross off the same room simultaneously -- one must wait for the other to finish writing. The search page in a travel website is like a lobby display board that updates every minute -- it might briefly show a room as available after the last one was just booked, but the actual reservation book (database) is always correct.

exercises.title: Build an Availability Calendar API
exercises.description: Create an API that returns a 30-day availability calendar for a given room type. Pre-populate the room_availability table with seed data. Support querying available rooms and prices for a date range. Include a visual ASCII calendar representation in the response.
exercises.hint: Generate rows with a script that creates one row per room_type per date for the next 365 days. Use GROUP BY to aggregate availability across dates. Add price variations for weekends.

exercises.title: Concurrent Booking Test
exercises.description: Set up a room with exactly 1 available room on a specific date. Write a load test that sends 50 concurrent booking requests for that room. Verify that exactly 1 booking succeeds and 49 fail gracefully with 'no availability' errors.
exercises.hint: Use Promise.all to fire all requests simultaneously. The SELECT FOR UPDATE lock ensures only one transaction succeeds. Assert the final available_rooms count is 0.

exercises.title: Dynamic Pricing Engine
exercises.description: Implement a pricing service that adjusts room rates based on: occupancy rate, day of week, season, and days until checkin. Write tests that verify prices increase as occupancy rises and weekend rates are higher than weekday rates.
exercises.hint: Use multiplier functions that compound: `basePrice * occupancyMultiplier * weekendMultiplier * seasonMultiplier`. Store adjustment rules in a config table for flexibility.

exercises.title: Search with Geolocation
exercises.description: Build a property search that supports 'hotels near me' using latitude/longitude. Index properties in Elasticsearch with geo_point fields. Return results sorted by distance with a radius filter.
exercises.hint: Use Elasticsearch's `geo_distance` filter and `geo_distance` sort. Index properties with `{ location: { lat, lon } }`. Support both city name search and coordinate-based search.

problems.problem: Double bookings occurring despite database constraints
problems.cause: The application reads availability and creates the booking in separate, non-transactional steps. Between the availability check and the booking insert, another request books the last room.
problems.solution: Use SELECT FOR UPDATE within a single database transaction. The lock prevents concurrent transactions from reading the same availability row until the first transaction commits or rolls back. Add a CHECK constraint (available_rooms >= 0) as a safety net.
problems.symptoms: Two confirmed bookings for the same room on the same night. Hotel reports overbooking. available_rooms column shows negative values.

problems.problem: Search results showing sold-out hotels as available
problems.cause: The availability cache (Redis, 60s TTL) still holds stale data after the last room was booked. Search results use cached data that has not yet expired.
problems.solution: Accept this as a trade-off: search showing briefly stale data is OK. Always re-check availability at booking time (with locking). Optionally, use cache invalidation on booking to reduce staleness. Show 'limited availability' warnings when available_rooms <= 3.

problems.problem: Slow search queries during peak booking season
problems.cause: The availability join query across millions of rows is expensive, especially when filtering by date range across many properties. Elasticsearch index is not properly tuned for concurrent queries.
problems.solution: Denormalize availability into the Elasticsearch index (update async via CDC). Add read replicas for the availability database. Use result caching with short TTL. Pre-compute popular search combinations (top cities + next 30 days).

further_reading.note: Real-world insights into building and scaling a hotel booking platform

further_reading.note: Deep coverage of transaction isolation levels and locking strategies used in booking systems

further_reading.title: Elasticsearch Geospatial Queries
further_reading.note: Official guide to geo_point and geo_shape queries for location-based hotel search

further_reading.title: Revenue Management and Dynamic Pricing
further_reading.note: Background on the pricing optimization strategies used by hotels and airlines

9.3.4.intuition: Think of buying concert tickets like entering a physical box office that only has 5 windows. When a hot show goes on sale, thousands of people show up. Instead of a mob rushing the windows, security sets up a line (the virtual queue). They hand out numbered tickets so everyone knows their position. Every few minutes, a batch of people is let into the lobby (admitted). In the lobby, you have 15 minutes to pick your seats at a kiosk (the hold). The kiosk puts a sticky note on your chosen seats so nobody else can grab them. If you do not pay within 15 minutes, the sticky notes are removed and those seats go back to available. This is exactly how the system works: the queue shapes traffic, holds are temporary claims, and the final purchase is the only permanent action.

exercises.title: Build a Virtual Queue
exercises.description: Implement a virtual queue system using Redis sorted sets. Support join, check position, and batch admission. Test with 10,000 simulated users joining the queue and being admitted in batches of 50 every 2 seconds. Track metrics: queue depth over time, average wait time, admission rate.
exercises.hint: Use ZADD with timestamp scores for ordering. Use ZRANGE + ZREM for batch admission. Use SISMEMBER for checking admission status. Run the queue processor as a setInterval loop.

exercises.title: Atomic Seat Hold with Redis
exercises.description: Build a seat hold service that uses Redis SET NX to ensure only one user can hold a seat at a time. Implement: hold acquisition (10 min TTL), hold release, hold extension, and automatic expiry. Test with 100 concurrent users trying to hold the same seat.
exercises.hint: Use `redis.set(key, value, 'EX', 600, 'NX')` for atomic hold. Return failure immediately if NX fails. Use a Lua script if you need to atomically hold multiple seats.

exercises.title: Seat Map Availability Service
exercises.description: Create an API that returns real-time seat availability for a venue section. Combine database state (SOLD) with Redis state (HELD) to produce the current availability view. Implement a WebSocket endpoint that pushes availability updates as seats are held or released.
exercises.hint: Query the database for sold seats, then check Redis for held seats, then merge. Use a Redis pub/sub channel to broadcast hold/release events to WebSocket subscribers.

problems.problem: Seat holds expiring during checkout, causing users to lose their tickets
problems.cause: The 10-minute hold timer expires while the user is entering payment information or the payment processor is slow. The seats return to available and another user grabs them.
problems.solution: Extend the hold when checkout begins (add 5 more minutes). Show a countdown timer on the client. Use a shorter initial hold (7 min for browsing) and extend on checkout initiation. Allow the user to re-hold the same seats if they expired within the last 60 seconds.
problems.symptoms: Users report completing payment but receiving 'seats no longer available' errors. High cart abandonment rate on the checkout page.

problems.problem: Queue position jumps erratically or users get pushed back in the queue
problems.cause: Using a Redis sorted set score based on timestamp, but clock skew between multiple application servers causes inconsistent ordering. Or the queue position is calculated incorrectly when batches are admitted.
problems.solution: Use a single atomic counter (INCR) for position assignment instead of timestamps. Alternatively, use a centralized time source. Calculate position as ZRANK relative to the remaining queue, not absolute position.

problems.problem: System crashes under load when a popular event goes on sale
problems.cause: Queue polling (every 2 seconds per user) creates massive read load on Redis. Without proper rate limiting, 10M users polling creates 5M QPS. The API gateway and Redis cannot handle the load.
problems.solution: Use longer polling intervals with jitter (2-5 seconds, randomized). Cache queue positions in local memory for 1 second. Use CDN for the waiting room page. Implement WebSocket for push-based position updates instead of client polling. Pre-warm Redis cluster before on-sale events.

further_reading.title: Ticketmaster Tech Blog: The Wait Room
further_reading.note: Insights into how Ticketmaster handles on-sale traffic spikes with virtual queues

further_reading.title: Redis SET NX Documentation
further_reading.note: The SET command with NX flag is the foundation of distributed locking used for seat holds

further_reading.title: Designing for Scale: High-Traffic Event Systems
further_reading.note: AWS case study on Ticketmaster's cloud architecture for handling millions of concurrent users

further_reading.title: Martin Kleppmann - Designing Data-Intensive Applications (Chapter 8: Distributed Systems)
further_reading.note: Covers distributed locking, clock skew, and consistency challenges relevant to real-time booking systems

9.3.5.intuition: Think of a food delivery platform as a three-way coordination problem, like an air traffic control tower. The customer is the departing passenger, the restaurant is the terminal kitchen, and the driver is the airplane. The dispatch engine is the control tower that must decide which plane to assign to which gate, considering where each plane is right now, how fast it is moving, which direction it is heading, and how long the food will take to prepare. The live tracking map is like the flight tracker -- it shows the customer exactly where their food is. The state machine (PLACED, PREPARING, PICKED_UP, DELIVERED) is like the flight status board (BOARDING, DEPARTED, IN FLIGHT, LANDED). Everything must happen within a tight time window because, unlike a package, food cannot sit on a shelf for days.

exercises.title: Build a Geospatial Driver Finder
exercises.description: Use Redis GEOADD and GEORADIUS to build a driver location service. Seed 1000 random driver locations within a city. Implement: add/update driver location, find nearest N drivers within a radius, and filter by availability status. Benchmark query performance with different numbers of drivers.
exercises.hint: Use `redis.geoadd('drivers', longitude, latitude, driverId)` to add locations. Use `redis.georadius('drivers', lng, lat, radius, 'km', 'WITHDIST', 'ASC', 'COUNT', 10)` to find nearby drivers. Store availability in a separate hash.

exercises.title: Order State Machine
exercises.description: Implement a robust order state machine with Express endpoints for each transition. Enforce valid transitions (e.g., cannot go from PLACED to DELIVERED). Log all transitions. Write tests that verify: valid transitions succeed, invalid transitions return 400, and concurrent transition attempts are handled correctly.
exercises.hint: Define a transition map as a constant object. Use database optimistic locking (check current status in WHERE clause of UPDATE) to prevent race conditions. Emit events on each transition.

exercises.title: Real-Time Order Tracking
exercises.description: Build a WebSocket server that pushes driver location updates and order status changes to connected customers. Use Redis Pub/Sub to distribute updates across multiple WebSocket server instances. Test with a simulated driver that moves along a path, updating location every 5 seconds.
exercises.hint: Use the `ws` library for WebSocket. Subscribe to Redis channels per order ID. Simulate driver movement by incrementing lat/lng values along a pre-defined route array.

exercises.title: ETA Estimation Service
exercises.description: Build an ETA service that estimates delivery time based on: restaurant prep time (from historical data), driver distance to restaurant (Haversine formula), and restaurant to customer distance. Test accuracy by comparing estimated vs actual delivery times with simulated data.
exercises.hint: Use the Haversine formula for distance calculation. Assume 25 km/h average urban speed. Add a buffer for restaurant queue depth. Compare with actual timestamps to calculate mean absolute error.

problems.problem: Drivers receiving delivery offers for restaurants that are far away, leading to high decline rates
problems.cause: The dispatch engine uses a fixed radius that is too large, or the scoring algorithm does not sufficiently weight distance. High decline rates mean orders wait longer for a driver, degrading customer experience.
problems.solution: Start with a small radius (2km) and gradually expand. Weight distance heavily in the scoring formula. Consider the driver's current heading -- a driver 3km away but heading toward the restaurant is better than one 1km away heading the opposite direction. Track acceptance rates per radius and tune dynamically.
problems.symptoms: Average driver acceptance rate below 50%. Orders waiting more than 10 minutes for driver assignment. Driver complaints about unprofitable long-distance pickups.

problems.problem: Real-time tracking showing stale or jumping driver positions
problems.cause: Location updates from the driver app are inconsistent due to GPS drift, cellular connectivity gaps, or the app being backgrounded by the OS. The client interpolates incorrectly between infrequent updates.
problems.solution: Implement client-side location smoothing (interpolate between known points). Detect anomalous jumps (> 500m in 5 seconds) and discard them. Use Kalman filtering on the server side to smooth GPS data. Show a wider accuracy circle when confidence is low. Fall back to road-snapping using a routing API.

problems.problem: ETA estimates are consistently inaccurate during peak hours
problems.cause: The ETA model uses static average speeds that do not account for traffic, weather, or restaurant being overwhelmed during dinner rush. Restaurant prep times increase as order volume rises but the model does not adapt.
problems.solution: Use dynamic prep time that increases with the restaurant's active order count. Incorporate real-time traffic data from a mapping API. Track historical ETA accuracy and apply correction factors by time of day and day of week. Add a restaurant-specific queue depth multiplier. Always show a range (e.g., '25-35 min') rather than a precise number.

further_reading.note: Technical deep dives on dispatch optimization, real-time tracking, and scaling a food delivery platform

further_reading.title: Redis Geospatial Commands
further_reading.note: Official documentation for GEOADD, GEORADIUS, and other geospatial commands used for driver location

further_reading.note: Covers geospatial indexing, time-series data, and the data modeling challenges present in delivery systems

further_reading.title: Uber's Dispatch System (H3 Geospatial Index)
further_reading.note: Uber's hexagonal hierarchical spatial index -- a more advanced approach to geospatial partitioning used in ride-sharing and delivery

9.4.1.intuition: Think of autocomplete like a librarian who has memorized the most popular book titles. When you say "har...", they immediately suggest "Harry Potter" without searching every shelf. The trie is like an organized card catalog where following letters down a path instantly narrows options, and the librarian has pre-sorted the most popular completions for every prefix they know.

exercises.title: Build a Trie with Top-K Caching
exercises.description: Implement an AutocompleteTrie class in JavaScript that inserts words with frequencies, pre-computes the top 5 suggestions at every node, and returns suggestions in O(prefix_length) time. Load it with 10,000 words from a dictionary file and benchmark lookup speed.
exercises.hint: After all insertions, call a recursive buildTopSuggestions() that traverses the entire trie bottom-up, collecting and sorting all completions at each node.

exercises.title: Redis-Backed Autocomplete Service
exercises.description: Build an Express API that stores autocomplete suggestions in Redis sorted sets. Implement endpoints to add queries with frequencies and retrieve top-k suggestions for a prefix. Use pipelining for batch inserts.
exercises.hint: Use ZADD to insert with scores and ZREVRANGE to retrieve top results. Store one sorted set per prefix (e.g., key 'ac:he' for prefix 'he').

exercises.title: Debounced Client with Request Cancellation
exercises.description: Create a frontend search input that debounces API calls by 200ms and cancels in-flight requests when new keystrokes arrive. Measure how many API calls are saved compared to calling on every keystroke.
exercises.hint: Use AbortController to cancel previous fetch requests. Track total keystrokes vs actual API calls to compute savings.

problems.problem: Autocomplete suggestions are stale and don't reflect trending topics
problems.cause: The trie is rebuilt too infrequently (e.g., daily) and the data pipeline has too much lag between query collection and trie updates.
problems.solution: Implement a two-tier system: a base trie rebuilt hourly from historical data, plus a real-time trending layer using a sliding window counter in Redis. Merge results at query time with a weighted scoring formula that boosts recent spikes.
problems.symptoms: A major news event occurs but related search suggestions don't appear for hours. Users notice autocomplete is "behind" compared to competitors.

problems.problem: Memory usage on trie servers keeps growing and eventually causes OOM crashes
problems.cause: The trie stores every unique query ever seen, including long-tail queries with very low frequency. Rare queries consume memory without providing useful suggestions.
problems.solution: Set a minimum frequency threshold (e.g., 10 searches/week) for trie inclusion. Periodically prune low-frequency entries. Use compact trie representations (compressed/Patricia trie) to reduce node count.

problems.problem: Latency spikes during trie rebuilds
problems.cause: The trie is rebuilt in-place on the serving node, causing garbage collection pressure and contention during the build process.
problems.solution: Build the new trie offline on a separate builder service. Serialize it to a binary snapshot, distribute the snapshot to serving nodes, and atomically swap the old trie for the new one. This ensures zero-downtime updates.

further_reading.note: Real-world implementation of a prefix search service with trie sharding

further_reading.note: Comprehensive walkthrough of the autocomplete system design problem

further_reading.note: Official Redis docs on sorted sets, the foundation for simple autocomplete systems

9.4.2.intuition: Think of a distributed task scheduler like a postal sorting center. Letters (tasks) arrive with delivery dates written on them. A sorting machine (scheduler) checks the clock, pulls out letters that are due for delivery, and distributes them to mail carriers (workers). If a carrier gets sick (worker fails), the letter is returned and given to another carrier. A supervisor (leader) coordinates the sorting so no letter gets sorted twice.

exercises.title: Build a Simple Task Scheduler with BullMQ
exercises.description: Create a task scheduling service using Express and BullMQ that accepts task definitions via REST API, stores them in PostgreSQL, and executes them at the scheduled time. Implement both one-time and recurring (cron) task types.
exercises.hint: Use BullMQ's delayed jobs feature for one-time tasks and the repeat option for cron tasks. Store task metadata in PostgreSQL for durability.

exercises.title: Implement Leader Election with Redis
exercises.description: Build a leader election mechanism using Redis SET NX with expiry. Run 3 Node.js processes that compete for leadership. Only the leader should poll for due tasks. Verify that leadership transfers when the leader crashes.
exercises.hint: Use SET key value PX ttl NX for atomic lock acquisition. Renew the lease at 1/3 of the TTL interval. Use a Lua script for safe lease renewal.

exercises.title: Retry Logic with Exponential Backoff
exercises.description: Implement a task executor that retries failed tasks with exponential backoff (1s, 2s, 4s, 8s, 16s, max 60s). Track retry counts, failure reasons, and final status in the database. Add a dead letter queue for tasks that exhaust all retries.
exercises.hint: Use BullMQ's built-in backoff option or implement manual retry scheduling by computing the next retry time and re-enqueuing.

exercises.title: Build a Task Dashboard API
exercises.description: Create API endpoints that return scheduler health metrics: tasks pending, tasks running, tasks completed in the last hour, tasks failed, average execution time, and queue depth. Use PostgreSQL aggregate queries.
exercises.hint: Use GROUP BY status with COUNT for task breakdowns. Use AVG(completed_at - started_at) for average execution time.

problems.problem: Tasks are being executed multiple times (duplicate execution)
problems.cause: Multiple scheduler nodes are polling for due tasks simultaneously without proper coordination, or a task completes but the status update fails, causing it to be picked up again.
problems.solution: Use leader election so only one scheduler polls at a time. Use SELECT ... FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED to atomically claim tasks. Implement idempotency keys on the consumer side so duplicate executions are harmless.
problems.symptoms: Webhook endpoints receive the same payload multiple times. Task execution logs show duplicate entries with overlapping timestamps.

problems.problem: Scheduled tasks are consistently late (missing their scheduled time by minutes)
problems.cause: The scheduler poll interval is too long, or the task queue is backed up because workers cannot process tasks fast enough.
problems.solution: Reduce poll interval to 1 second. Monitor queue depth and auto-scale workers based on queue size. Use priority queues so high-priority tasks are not blocked by low-priority ones.

problems.problem: Database connection pool exhaustion during peak scheduling periods
problems.cause: Each worker holds a database connection while executing long-running tasks, and the connection pool runs out when many tasks execute concurrently.
problems.solution: Separate the task execution (webhook call) from the database operations. Use a dedicated small pool for status updates. Consider batching status updates with a write-behind buffer.

further_reading.note: Detailed walkthrough of distributed task scheduler design considerations

9.4.3.intuition: A web crawler is like a team of librarians exploring an infinite library. Each librarian (worker) picks a room (URL) from a shared to-do list (frontier), reads the books (downloads content), writes down references to other rooms (extracts links), and adds new rooms to the to-do list. A coordinator ensures no two librarians visit the same room, and everyone waits politely before re-entering any building (domain) they recently visited.

exercises.title: Build a Basic Web Crawler
exercises.description: Create a Node.js web crawler that starts from a seed URL, fetches pages, extracts links using cheerio, and follows them up to depth 3. Implement a visited set to avoid re-crawling. Limit to a single domain. Output the site map.
exercises.hint: Use a BFS approach with a queue. Track visited URLs in a Set. Use new URL(href, baseURL) to resolve relative links.

exercises.title: Implement a Politeness Layer
exercises.description: Extend the basic crawler to respect robots.txt rules and enforce a 1-second delay between requests to the same domain. Use a per-domain queue with timestamps to track last request time.
exercises.hint: Fetch and parse robots.txt before crawling any domain. Use a Map<domain, lastRequestTime> and await a delay if less than 1 second has passed.

exercises.title: URL Deduplication at Scale
exercises.description: Implement a Bloom filter-based URL deduplicator. Insert 1 million random URLs into it and measure false positive rate. Compare memory usage vs a Set-based approach.
exercises.hint: Use the 'bloom-filters' npm package or implement a simple Bloom filter with multiple hash functions. A Set of 1M URLs uses ~200MB vs a Bloom filter using ~2MB.

problems.problem: Crawler gets stuck in URL traps (infinite URLs from the same domain)
problems.cause: Calendar pages, search result pages, or dynamically generated paths create infinite URL variations (e.g., /calendar?date=2026-01-01, /calendar?date=2026-01-02, ...).
problems.solution: Implement max URL depth per domain, detect repeating URL patterns with regex, limit pages per domain, and use URL fingerprinting that ignores query parameter values to detect structural duplicates.
problems.symptoms: The frontier grows unboundedly for certain domains. The crawler spends all its time on a few domains while ignoring others.

problems.problem: DNS resolution becomes a bottleneck at high crawl rates
problems.cause: Each page fetch requires a DNS lookup, and the default system resolver cannot handle 15,000 lookups per second. DNS timeouts cause cascading slowdowns.
problems.solution: Implement an in-process DNS cache with TTL. Use a dedicated DNS resolver (like dnsmasq) on each crawler node. Pre-resolve DNS for domains in the frontier. Batch DNS lookups.

problems.problem: Downloaded content consumes storage faster than expected
problems.cause: No deduplication of page content across URLs, and no filtering of low-value pages (error pages, login walls, pagination duplicates).
problems.solution: Use content hashing (SHA-256) to detect exact duplicates and SimHash for near-duplicates. Filter out pages below a minimum content threshold. Compress stored HTML with gzip/brotli.
