9.4.4.intuition: Think of a CDN like a chain of convenience stores. Instead of everyone driving to the central warehouse (origin server) for a bottle of water, each neighborhood has a local store (edge PoP) stocked with the most popular items. When a store runs out, it calls the regional distributor (shield), who only goes to the warehouse if they are also out. Cache invalidation is like a product recall: a message goes out to every store to remove the item from shelves.

exercises.title: Build an Edge Cache with LRU and TTL
exercises.description: Implement an in-memory edge cache server using Express and lru-cache that proxies requests to an origin server, caches responses based on Cache-Control headers, and returns X-Cache: HIT or MISS headers. Measure cache hit ratio over 1000 requests.
exercises.hint: Use the lru-cache npm package with maxSize and ttl options. Parse the Cache-Control max-age directive to set per-item TTL.

exercises.title: Implement Cache Invalidation with Pub/Sub
exercises.description: Create 3 edge cache instances that subscribe to a Redis Pub/Sub channel. Build a purge API that publishes invalidation messages, and verify all 3 caches purge the content within seconds.
exercises.hint: Use ioredis subscribe/publish for the pub/sub channel. Each edge instance subscribes on startup and purges matching keys on message receipt.

exercises.title: GeoDNS Simulator
exercises.description: Build a DNS-like router that maps client IP addresses to the nearest edge server based on geographic coordinates. Use the geoip-lite npm package to resolve IPs to locations, then compute the nearest edge from a list of PoP coordinates using the Haversine formula.
exercises.hint: Pre-define edge PoP coordinates (e.g., US-East: 39.0, -77.5). Use the Haversine formula to compute distance between the client's geolocated coordinates and each PoP.

problems.problem: Cache hit ratio is below 50%, causing excessive origin load
problems.cause: Cache keys include unnecessary variation (e.g., unique query parameters, tracking cookies, or session headers), causing each request to generate a unique cache key even for identical content.
problems.solution: Normalize cache keys by stripping tracking parameters (utm_*, fbclid, etc.). Configure which headers and query parameters are included in the cache key. Use the Vary header correctly to avoid over-segmentation.
problems.symptoms: Origin server is overloaded despite having a CDN. X-Cache headers show mostly MISS. Identical content is stored multiple times under different cache keys.

problems.problem: Stale content served after origin update
problems.cause: Cache TTL is too long and no purge was issued after the content update. Or the purge did not propagate to all edge servers.
problems.solution: Implement cache busting with content hashes in filenames (e.g., app.abc123.js). For dynamic content, use shorter TTLs with stale-while-revalidate. Set up automatic purge triggers on content deployment.

problems.problem: Thundering herd on cache expiration
problems.cause: A popular item's TTL expires simultaneously on many edge servers, causing all of them to request it from the origin at the same time, overwhelming the origin.
problems.solution: Implement request coalescing (only one origin request per cache key, others wait). Add jitter to TTLs so they don't expire at the same time. Use stale-while-revalidate to serve stale content while refreshing in the background.

9.4.5.intuition: Think of a metrics system like a network of weather stations. Each station (agent) measures temperature, wind, and humidity (metrics) every few seconds and sends readings to a central weather service (ingestion pipeline). The service stores fine-grained data for recent days but only daily averages for years past (downsampling). Meteorologists (users) view dashboards of charts and set up storm warnings (alerts) that trigger when thresholds are crossed. The key challenge is that there are millions of stations reporting simultaneously.

exercises.title: Build a Metrics Agent and Collector
exercises.description: Create a MetricsAgent class that collects CPU, memory, and custom application metrics, buffers them, and sends them in batches to a collector Express server. The collector should store metrics in a PostgreSQL table and expose a simple query endpoint.
exercises.hint: Use os.cpus() and os.freemem() for system metrics. Buffer data points in an array and flush every 10 seconds via HTTP POST. Use COPY for efficient batch inserts.

exercises.title: Implement Time-Series Downsampling
exercises.description: Write a downsampler that reads raw 10-second metrics from a PostgreSQL table, computes 1-minute rollups (avg, min, max, count), and writes them to a rollup table. Run it periodically and verify the rollups match expected values.
exercises.hint: Use time_bucket('1 minute', timestamp) if using TimescaleDB, or date_trunc('minute', timestamp) for standard PostgreSQL. GROUP BY the bucket and series_id.

exercises.title: Build an Alert Evaluator
exercises.description: Create an alerting engine that evaluates rules defined in a database against recent metric values. When a threshold is breached, create an incident and log a notification. When the value returns to normal, resolve the incident.
exercises.hint: Query the last N minutes of data for each alert rule's metric. Compare the aggregated value against the threshold. Track incident state to avoid duplicate alerts.

exercises.title: Dashboard Query API
exercises.description: Build a query API that accepts a metric name, time range, granularity, and tag filters, then returns time-series data points. Automatically select the appropriate rollup table based on the time range (raw for <1h, 1m for <24h, 1h for >24h).
exercises.hint: Use a routing function: if (timeRange < 1 hour) query metrics_raw; else if (timeRange < 24 hours) query metrics_1m; else query metrics_1h.

problems.problem: Ingestion pipeline falls behind, causing stale dashboards and delayed alerts
problems.cause: Write workers cannot keep up with the incoming data rate. Kafka consumer lag grows, meaning metrics are ingested but not yet written to the TSDB.
problems.solution: Scale write workers horizontally (more consumer instances with more Kafka partitions). Use batch inserts instead of individual writes. Pre-aggregate on the agent side (send 1-minute rollups instead of raw points). Monitor Kafka consumer lag as a key operational metric.
problems.symptoms: Dashboards show data gaps or delays. Alerts fire minutes after the actual threshold breach. Kafka consumer lag metric is growing continuously.

problems.problem: Query latency degrades as the number of unique time series grows (high cardinality explosion)
problems.cause: A tag with unbounded values (like user_id, request_id, or full URL path) creates millions of unique time series, making index lookups and aggregation slow.
problems.solution: Enforce cardinality limits per metric (e.g., max 100K unique series). Reject or drop metrics that exceed the limit. Educate users to avoid high-cardinality tags. Use pre-aggregation for high-cardinality dimensions.
problems.symptoms: Dashboard queries that were fast become increasingly slow over days/weeks. Series registry table grows to hundreds of millions of rows. Memory usage on TSDB nodes keeps climbing.

problems.problem: Alerts are flapping (firing and resolving rapidly in alternation)
problems.cause: The metric value oscillates around the threshold, causing the alert to fire and resolve on every evaluation cycle. This floods notification channels with noise.
problems.solution: Implement hysteresis: use a higher threshold to fire and a lower threshold to resolve (or vice versa). Add a minimum firing duration (e.g., must be above threshold for 3 consecutive evaluations). Support 'for' clauses like Prometheus (e.g., fire only if condition is true for 5 minutes).

further_reading.note: Prometheus is the de facto open-source metrics system; understanding its architecture is essential

9.5.1.intuition: Think of a social graph like a city road map. Each person is an intersection, and each friendship is a road connecting two intersections. Finding mutual friends is like asking which intersections can be reached directly from both of two given intersections. Finding degrees of separation is like finding the shortest driving route between two points. At city scale you need a good index (GPS/map), not brute-force exploration of every road.

exercises.title: Build an Adjacency List Service
exercises.description: Implement a Node.js service with Prisma that stores a social graph as an adjacency list in PostgreSQL. Support adding connections, removing connections, and listing a user's friends with cursor-based pagination.
exercises.hint: Store edges in both directions (user_id, friend_id) for O(1) lookup. Use a transaction to insert both rows atomically.

exercises.title: Redis Mutual Friends
exercises.description: Load each user's friend list into a Redis sorted set (ZADD). Implement a mutual friends endpoint using ZINTERSTORE. Benchmark it against a pure SQL JOIN approach for users with 500+ connections.
exercises.hint: Use ioredis pipeline to batch-load friend sets. Compare execution time with console.time for both approaches.

exercises.title: Bidirectional BFS
exercises.description: Implement a bidirectional BFS algorithm in JavaScript that finds the shortest path between two users in an in-memory graph of 10,000 nodes. Compare its performance against a standard BFS.
exercises.hint: Maintain two visited sets expanding from each end. At each step, expand the smaller frontier. When frontiers overlap, you have found the shortest path.

exercises.title: Friend Suggestion Pipeline
exercises.description: Build a BullMQ worker that pre-computes friend-of-friend suggestions for each user. Store the top 50 suggestions in Redis. Expose an API that reads from this cache with a fallback to live computation.
exercises.hint: Process users in batches of 1000. Use the SQL friends-of-friends query with COUNT and GROUP BY to rank suggestions.

problems.problem: Mutual friends query takes 5+ seconds for users with many connections
problems.cause: SQL JOIN between two large adjacency lists is O(n*m) without proper indexing, and popular users (influencers) can have millions of connections.
problems.solution: Use Redis sorted sets with ZINTERSTORE for sub-millisecond set intersection. For extremely popular users, pre-compute and cache mutual friend lists for the most common pairs. Add a composite index on (user_id, friend_id) for the SQL fallback path.

problems.problem: Friend suggestion computation overloads the database during peak hours
problems.cause: Computing friends-of-friends in real-time requires multiple JOINs across millions of rows, creating heavy read load on the primary database.
problems.solution: Move suggestion computation to an offline batch pipeline that runs during off-peak hours. Use read replicas for the computation. Store results in Redis with a 1-hour TTL. Serve suggestions from cache with a fallback to a simpler algorithm (e.g., same company/school) when cache is cold.

problems.problem: Adding a connection is slow because it requires updating multiple stores
problems.cause: A single connection add must write to the connections table, adjacency list (both directions), invalidate caches for both users, update suggestion caches, and update Elasticsearch.
problems.solution: Make the critical path synchronous (adjacency list insert + cache invalidation) and push secondary updates (suggestions, search index) to a message queue (BullMQ). Return 201 immediately after the database write. Process secondary effects asynchronously.

9.5.2.intuition: Think of a ride-sharing system like a pizza delivery dispatch center. Customers call in orders (ride requests), the dispatcher (matching service) looks at a map of available drivers (delivery drivers) and picks the closest one. The map is constantly updating as drivers move around. During the Super Bowl (surge), there are way more orders than drivers, so prices go up to attract more drivers to work. The whole system is a two-sided marketplace where you must balance supply and demand in real-time across geography.

exercises.title: Geospatial Matching with Redis
exercises.description: Build a Node.js service that stores driver locations using Redis GEOADD and finds the 5 nearest drivers to a pickup point using GEOSEARCH. Simulate 1000 drivers in a city grid and benchmark query latency.
exercises.hint: Use ioredis GEOADD to seed random driver positions within a bounding box. Use GEOSEARCH FROMLONLAT BYRADIUS to find nearby drivers.

exercises.title: Ride State Machine
exercises.description: Implement a ride lifecycle state machine in Node.js. Define valid state transitions, reject invalid ones, and emit events (via EventEmitter) on each transition. Persist state to PostgreSQL via Prisma.
exercises.hint: Define a transitions map: { requested: ['matching', 'cancelled'], matching: ['driver_assigned', 'cancelled'], ... }. Validate before updating.

exercises.title: Surge Pricing Calculator
exercises.description: Build a surge pricing service that divides a city into geohash cells, counts ride requests and available drivers per cell, and computes a surge multiplier. Update every 30 seconds using a BullMQ repeatable job.
exercises.hint: Use the ngeohash npm package. Track demand with Redis INCR on geohash keys with TTL. Count supply with GEOSEARCH per cell.

problems.problem: Two riders get matched with the same driver simultaneously
problems.cause: Race condition in the matching service — two concurrent ride requests both see the same driver as available and attempt to assign them.
problems.solution: Use Redis SET with NX (set-if-not-exists) as a distributed lock on the driver ID. Only the first request that acquires the lock gets the driver. The second request must fall back to the next candidate. This provides atomic check-and-set semantics without database-level locking.

problems.problem: Driver location updates are lagging behind by 10-15 seconds
problems.cause: WebSocket connection drops and reconnects, or the location service is overwhelmed by write volume at peak hours.
problems.solution: Use a dedicated Redis cluster for location writes, separate from the matching Redis. Buffer location updates client-side and send in batches (every 3-4 seconds). Implement WebSocket heartbeat with automatic reconnection. Accept slightly stale locations for matching (5s staleness is acceptable).

problems.problem: Surge pricing oscillates rapidly, confusing riders
problems.cause: Supply/demand ratio is recomputed too frequently and small fluctuations cause large multiplier swings.
problems.solution: Smooth the surge multiplier using a moving average over the last 5 minutes instead of instantaneous values. Apply a dampening factor so the multiplier can only change by +/- 0.25 per computation cycle. Use hysteresis: surge only increases when demand/supply exceeds the threshold for 2 consecutive cycles.

9.5.3.intuition: Think of an online code editor like a shared art studio where each artist (user) gets their own private booth (sandbox container) with supplies (CPU, memory, packages). The canvas (editor) is visible to collaborators through a magic mirror (CRDT replication) that shows everyone's brushstrokes in real-time without conflicts. The building manager (Kubernetes) assigns booths on demand, cleans up after artists leave, and adds more floors to the building when it gets crowded.

exercises.title: Build a Code Execution Sandbox
exercises.description: Create a Node.js service that receives code (JavaScript), spawns it in a child_process with resource limits (timeout, memory), captures stdout/stderr, and returns the result. Prevent dangerous operations like file system access and network calls.
exercises.hint: Use child_process.fork() with a timeout. Use Node.js --max-old-space-size flag for memory limits. Create a restricted execution wrapper using vm2 or isolated-vm npm packages.

exercises.title: Real-Time Collaboration with Yjs
exercises.description: Build a minimal collaborative text editor using Yjs and WebSocket. Two browser tabs should be able to edit the same document simultaneously with changes appearing in real-time. Display remote cursors with different colors.
exercises.hint: Use y-websocket for the server transport layer and y-monaco or y-codemirror for editor binding. Use awareness protocol for cursor positions.

exercises.title: File Versioning System
exercises.description: Implement a file versioning system that stores content-addressable file snapshots (SHA-256 hash as key, content as value in S3). Create project snapshots as a manifest of file hashes. Support listing versions and restoring to any snapshot.
exercises.hint: Hash file content with crypto.createHash('sha256'). Store content in S3 with the hash as key (automatic deduplication). Store manifests in PostgreSQL with JSONB.

exercises.title: Terminal Streaming over WebSocket
exercises.description: Build a WebSocket server that spawns a shell process (bash) in a container and streams the PTY (pseudo-terminal) output to a connected browser client using xterm.js. Handle bidirectional I/O: user input from browser to shell, shell output to browser.
exercises.hint: Use the node-pty npm package to create a pseudo-terminal. Pipe pty.onData to WebSocket send, and WebSocket messages to pty.write.

problems.problem: Sandbox cold start takes 5+ seconds, making the run button feel slow
problems.cause: Pulling container images, initializing the filesystem, and installing dependencies on every sandbox creation adds significant latency.
problems.solution: Pre-warm a pool of generic sandbox containers (one per language) that are already running and waiting for assignment. When a user clicks 'Run', assign an idle container from the pool and sync project files into it. Replenish the pool asynchronously. For frequently used templates (Node.js, Python), keep 100+ pre-warmed containers. This reduces cold start from 5s to under 500ms.

problems.problem: CRDT document grows unboundedly in memory for long-lived editing sessions
problems.cause: Yjs stores the full history of operations for conflict resolution. Long editing sessions with many edits create large CRDT documents (100MB+) that consume excessive server memory.
problems.solution: Periodically compact the CRDT document by creating a fresh snapshot. Every N operations or every M minutes, encode the current state, create a new Y.Doc from that state, and replace the old document. Persist the snapshot to S3. On reconnection, clients load the latest snapshot rather than replaying the full history.

problems.problem: Users can escape the sandbox and access host resources
problems.cause: Standard Docker containers share the host kernel, and privilege escalation vulnerabilities can allow container escape.
problems.solution: Layer multiple isolation mechanisms: (1) Run containers with non-root user and drop all capabilities, (2) Use seccomp profiles to restrict system calls, (3) Apply AppArmor/SELinux profiles, (4) Use gVisor (user-space kernel) or Firecracker microVMs for kernel-level isolation, (5) Network policies to prevent sandbox-to-sandbox communication. Defense in depth is essential.

further_reading.title: Replit Engineering Blog: How We Run User Code
further_reading.note: How Replit uses Nix for reproducible sandbox environments

further_reading.title: Yjs: A CRDT Framework for Shared Editing
further_reading.note: Official documentation for the Yjs CRDT library used in collaborative editors

further_reading.title: Firecracker: Lightweight Virtualization for Serverless
further_reading.note: AWS's open-source microVM technology used for secure sandbox isolation

further_reading.title: Monaco Editor Documentation
further_reading.note: The VS Code editor component used in most online code editors

9.5.4.intuition: Imagine two people writing on the same whiteboard from different rooms, each with a camera feed of the board. With OT, a coordinator in the middle watches both cameras and adjusts each person's instructions so they do not overwrite each other — 'You wrote at position 5, but the other person already inserted text at position 3, so your position is actually 6 now.' With CRDTs, each person's marker has a unique invisible ID embedded in every letter, so the whiteboard itself knows how to merge any combination of edits without a coordinator. CRDTs are like self-organizing text.

exercises.title: Build an OT Engine
exercises.description: Implement a basic Operational Transformation server in Node.js that handles concurrent insert and delete operations from two clients. Verify that both clients converge to the same document state regardless of operation arrival order.
exercises.hint: Start with just insert operations. The key transform rule: if insert A is at position <= insert B's position, B's position shifts by A's text length. Build up to handling delete+insert and delete+delete pairs.

exercises.title: Collaborative Editor with Yjs
exercises.description: Build a collaborative rich text editor using Yjs, y-websocket, and Quill (or Monaco). Support bold, italic, and headings. Show remote cursors with user names. Persist the document to PostgreSQL using periodic snapshots.
exercises.hint: Use Y.XmlFragment for rich text. Bind to Quill with y-quill. The y-websocket package provides a ready-made WebSocket server. Snapshot with Y.encodeStateAsUpdate() every 30 seconds.

exercises.title: Operations Log with Snapshot Recovery
exercises.description: Implement a document persistence system that stores every operation in an append-only PostgreSQL table and creates snapshots every 100 operations. Build a recovery function that loads the latest snapshot and replays subsequent operations to reconstruct the document.
exercises.hint: Use a BIGSERIAL version column. Query: SELECT * FROM operations WHERE document_id = $1 AND version > $2 ORDER BY version ASC. Measure reconstruction time with and without snapshots.

problems.problem: Document takes 10+ seconds to load because the operations log has millions of entries
problems.cause: Without snapshots, loading a document requires replaying every operation since creation. A heavily-edited document can have millions of operations.
problems.solution: Implement periodic snapshotting — every 100-500 operations, serialize the current document state and store it. On load, fetch the latest snapshot and replay only the operations since that snapshot. This bounds loading time to O(snapshot_interval) operations regardless of total document history. Archive old operations to cold storage.

problems.problem: Cursor positions jump around when multiple users are typing near each other
problems.cause: Cursor positions are stored as absolute character offsets. When another user inserts text before the cursor, the offset becomes stale and points to the wrong position.
problems.solution: Use CRDT-relative positions for cursor tracking instead of absolute offsets. In Yjs, use Y.createRelativePositionFromTypeIndex() which creates a position that is stable relative to the surrounding characters, not the absolute offset. This position automatically adjusts as text is inserted or deleted around it.

problems.problem: WebSocket server crashes when a viral document gets 500+ simultaneous editors
problems.cause: Each editor generates ~3 operations/second. With 500 editors, that is 1,500 ops/sec inbound, each of which must be broadcast to 499 other clients — resulting in ~750,000 outbound messages/second on a single server.
problems.solution: Implement operation batching: collect operations in a 50ms window and send them as a single batch. This reduces message count by 10-50x. For extremely hot documents, implement a fan-out tree: the primary server sends updates to 5 relay servers, each of which handles 100 clients. Rate-limit cursor updates to 5/second per user. Consider read-only mode for viewers to eliminate their operation overhead.

further_reading.title: Operational Transformation (Wikipedia)
further_reading.note: Comprehensive overview of OT theory, algorithms, and history

further_reading.title: CRDTs: The Hard Parts (Martin Kleppmann)
further_reading.note: Excellent talk covering CRDT challenges in real collaborative editing scenarios

further_reading.title: Yjs Documentation
further_reading.note: The leading CRDT framework for collaborative editing in JavaScript

further_reading.title: How Google Docs Works (Real-Time Collaboration)
further_reading.note: Google's original blog post explaining their OT-based collaboration approach

9.6.1.body:
# Design a Distributed Message Queue (Kafka)

## Introduction

A distributed message queue is the central nervous system of modern data-intensive applications. It decouples producers from consumers, absorbs traffic spikes, guarantees delivery, and enables real-time data pipelines. In this lesson we design a system inspired by Apache Kafka — the industry standard for high-throughput, fault-tolerant event streaming.

## Requirements

### Functional Requirements

- Producers publish messages to named **topics**
- Consumers subscribe to topics and receive messages in order
- Messages are persisted durably (survive broker restarts)
- Support **consumer groups** — multiple consumers share the load within a group, but each message is delivered to exactly one consumer per group
- Support message **retention** by time or size (e.g., keep 7 days or 100 GB)
- Consumers can **replay** messages from any offset
- At-least-once delivery guarantee by default; support exactly-once semantics

### Non-Functional Requirements

- Throughput: 500,000 messages/second sustained
- Latency: p99 < 10 ms for produce, < 50 ms for end-to-end
- Durability: zero message loss for acknowledged writes
- Availability: 99.99% uptime
- Horizontal scalability: add brokers to increase capacity linearly

## Capacity Estimation

```
Messages/sec:        500,000
Average message size: 1 KB
Data ingestion rate:  500,000 * 1 KB = ~500 MB/s
Replication factor:   3
Actual write rate:    500 MB/s * 3 = 1.5 GB/s
Retention:            7 days
Storage per day:      500 MB/s * 86,400 = ~43 TB/day
Total storage (7d):   43 TB * 7 = ~300 TB (before replication)
With replication:     300 TB * 3 = ~900 TB
Broker count:         If each broker has 12 TB SSD, need ~75 brokers
                      Round up to 80 for headroom
```

## High-Level Architecture

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        Producers                                 │
│  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐        │
│  │ Service A │  │ Service B │  │ Service C │  │ Service D │       │
│  └─────┬─────┘  └─────┬─────┘  └─────┬─────┘  └─────┬─────┘    │
└────────┼──────────────┼──────────────┼──────────────┼───────────┘
         │              │              │              │
         ▼              ▼              ▼              ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                     Load Balancer / DNS                           │
└────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────┘
                             │
         ┌───────────────────┼───────────────────┐
         ▼                   ▼                   ▼
┌─────────────────┐ ┌─────────────────┐ ┌─────────────────┐
│   Broker 1      │ │   Broker 2      │ │   Broker 3      │
│                 │ │                 │ │                 │
│ Topic-A Part-0  │ │ Topic-A Part-1  │ │ Topic-A Part-2  │
│ Topic-B Part-1  │ │ Topic-B Part-2  │ │ Topic-B Part-0  │
│                 │ │                 │ │                 │
│ Commit Log      │ │ Commit Log      │ │ Commit Log      │
│ (Append-only)   │ │ (Append-only)   │ │ (Append-only)   │
└────────┬────────┘ └────────┬────────┘ └────────┬────────┘
         │                   │                   │
         └───────────┬───────┘───────────────────┘
                     │  Replication
         ┌───────────┼───────────────────┐
         ▼           ▼                   ▼
┌─────────────────┐ ┌─────────────────┐ ┌─────────────────┐
│   Replica 1     │ │   Replica 2     │ │   Replica 3     │
└─────────────────┘ └─────────────────┘ └─────────────────┘
                             │
         ┌───────────────────┼───────────────────┐
         ▼                   ▼                   ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        Consumers                                 │
│  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐           │
│  │Consumer Grp A │  │Consumer Grp B │  │Consumer Grp C │          │
│  │ C1  C2  C3   │  │ C1  C2       │  │ C1            │          │
│  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘           │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                   Coordination Service                           │
│                  (ZooKeeper / KRaft)                              │
│  - Broker membership      - Partition leadership                 │
│  - Consumer group offsets  - Topic configuration                 │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## Core Concepts

### Topics and Partitions

A **topic** is a named stream of records. Each topic is divided into **partitions** — ordered, immutable sequences of records. Partitions enable parallelism: each partition can be consumed by one consumer in a group.

```
Topic: user-events (3 partitions)

Partition 0: [msg0] [msg3] [msg6] [msg9]  ...
Partition 1: [msg1] [msg4] [msg7] [msg10] ...
Partition 2: [msg2] [msg5] [msg8] [msg11] ...

Each message has an offset (sequential ID within partition)
```

### Commit Log

Each partition is backed by an append-only **commit log** on disk. This is the key insight that makes Kafka fast — sequential disk writes are faster than random memory access on modern hardware.

```
Segment files on disk:

partition-0/
  00000000000000000000.log    (0 - 1,000,000)
  00000000000001000000.log    (1,000,001 - 2,000,000)
  00000000000002000000.log    (2,000,001 - ...)
  00000000000000000000.index  (offset -> position mapping)
  00000000000000000000.timeindex (timestamp -> offset mapping)
```

## API Design

### Producer API

```javascript
import express from 'express';
const app = express();
app.use(express.json());

// POST /api/v1/topics/:topicName/messages
// Publish a message to a topic
app.post('/api/v1/topics/:topicName/messages', async (req, res) => {
  const { topicName } = req.params;
  const { key, value, headers } = req.body;
  // key is used for partition assignment (hash(key) % numPartitions)
  // value is the message payload (Buffer or JSON)
  // headers are optional key-value metadata

  const result = await messageQueue.produce({
    topic: topicName,
    key,
    value: Buffer.from(JSON.stringify(value)),
    headers,
    timestamp: Date.now(),
  });

  res.status(201).json({
    topic: topicName,
    partition: result.partition,
    offset: result.offset,
    timestamp: result.timestamp,
  });
});

// POST /api/v1/topics/:topicName/messages/batch
// Publish a batch of messages
app.post('/api/v1/topics/:topicName/messages/batch', async (req, res) => {
  const { topicName } = req.params;
  const { messages } = req.body; // Array of { key, value, headers }

  const results = await messageQueue.produceBatch({
    topic: topicName,
    messages: messages.map(m => ({
      key: m.key,
      value: Buffer.from(JSON.stringify(m.value)),
      headers: m.headers,
      timestamp: Date.now(),
    })),
  });

  res.status(201).json({ results });
});
```

### Consumer API

```javascript
// POST /api/v1/consumers
// Register a consumer in a group
app.post('/api/v1/consumers', async (req, res) => {
  const { groupId, topics, autoCommit } = req.body;

  const consumer = await messageQueue.createConsumer({
    groupId,
    topics,
    autoCommit: autoCommit ?? true,
    sessionTimeout: 30000,
    heartbeatInterval: 3000,
  });

  res.status(201).json({
    consumerId: consumer.id,
    groupId,
    assignedPartitions: consumer.assignments,
  });
});

// GET /api/v1/consumers/:consumerId/messages
// Poll for new messages
app.get('/api/v1/consumers/:consumerId/messages', async (req, res) => {
  const { consumerId } = req.params;
  const maxMessages = parseInt(req.query.max) || 100;
  const timeoutMs = parseInt(req.query.timeout) || 5000;

  const messages = await messageQueue.poll(consumerId, {
    maxMessages,
    timeoutMs,
  });

  res.json({
    messages: messages.map(m => ({
      topic: m.topic,
      partition: m.partition,
      offset: m.offset,
      key: m.key,
      value: JSON.parse(m.value.toString()),
      timestamp: m.timestamp,
      headers: m.headers,
    })),
  });
});

// POST /api/v1/consumers/:consumerId/offsets
// Commit consumer offsets
app.post('/api/v1/consumers/:consumerId/offsets', async (req, res) => {
  const { consumerId } = req.params;
  const { offsets } = req.body;
  // offsets: [{ topic, partition, offset }]

  await messageQueue.commitOffsets(consumerId, offsets);
  res.status(204).send();
});
```

### Topic Management API

```javascript
// POST /api/v1/topics
app.post('/api/v1/topics', async (req, res) => {
  const { name, partitions, replicationFactor, retentionMs, retentionBytes } = req.body;

  const topic = await messageQueue.createTopic({
    name,
    partitions: partitions || 12,
    replicationFactor: replicationFactor || 3,
    config: {
      'retention.ms': retentionMs || 7 * 24 * 60 * 60 * 1000,
      'retention.bytes': retentionBytes || -1, // unlimited
      'segment.bytes': 1073741824, // 1 GB segments
    },
  });

  res.status(201).json(topic);
});
```

## Database Schema

The message queue primarily uses an append-only log on the filesystem, but metadata is stored in a coordination service. Here is the metadata schema:

```sql
-- Topic metadata
CREATE TABLE topics (
  id              UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
  name            VARCHAR(255) UNIQUE NOT NULL,
  partition_count INT NOT NULL DEFAULT 12,
  replication_factor INT NOT NULL DEFAULT 3,
  retention_ms    BIGINT NOT NULL DEFAULT 604800000,
  retention_bytes BIGINT DEFAULT -1,
  created_at      TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
  updated_at      TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW()
);

-- Partition assignments (which broker owns which partition)
CREATE TABLE partition_assignments (
  id              UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
  topic_id        UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES topics(id),
  partition_num   INT NOT NULL,
  leader_broker   VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
  replica_brokers TEXT[] NOT NULL,  -- array of broker IDs
  isr             TEXT[] NOT NULL,  -- in-sync replicas
  created_at      TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
  UNIQUE (topic_id, partition_num)
);

-- Consumer group offsets
CREATE TABLE consumer_offsets (
  group_id        VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
  topic_id        UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES topics(id),
  partition_num   INT NOT NULL,
  committed_offset BIGINT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  metadata        VARCHAR(255),
  committed_at    TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
  PRIMARY KEY (group_id, topic_id, partition_num)
);

-- Consumer group membership
CREATE TABLE consumer_group_members (
  id              UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
  group_id        VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
  consumer_id     VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
  assigned_partitions JSONB NOT NULL DEFAULT '[]',
  last_heartbeat  TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
  session_timeout_ms INT NOT NULL DEFAULT 30000,
  joined_at       TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
  UNIQUE (group_id, consumer_id)
);

CREATE INDEX idx_consumer_group_heartbeat
  ON consumer_group_members(group_id, last_heartbeat);

-- Broker registry
CREATE TABLE brokers (
  id              VARCHAR(255) PRIMARY KEY,
  host            VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
  port            INT NOT NULL,
  rack            VARCHAR(50),
  status          VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL DEFAULT 'active',
  last_heartbeat  TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
  registered_at   TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW()
);
```

## Deep Dive: Key Design Decisions

### Why Append-Only Logs?

Sequential I/O on modern SSDs achieves 500+ MB/s, while random I/O achieves ~50 MB/s. By only appending to logs, we exploit the disk's strongest access pattern.

### Zero-Copy Transfer

When a consumer reads messages, the broker uses `sendfile()` to transfer data directly from the OS page cache to the network socket, bypassing user space entirely.

```
Traditional:   Disk -> Kernel Buffer -> User Buffer -> Socket Buffer -> NIC
Zero-copy:     Disk -> Kernel Buffer ─────────────────────────────> NIC
```

### Consumer Group Rebalancing

When a consumer joins or leaves a group, partitions must be reassigned. This is the rebalancing protocol:

```
1. Consumer C3 joins Group-A
2. Coordinator detects membership change
3. All consumers in Group-A pause
4. Coordinator runs assignment algorithm:
   - Range: P0,P1 -> C1; P2,P3 -> C2; P4,P5 -> C3
   - Round Robin: P0->C1, P1->C2, P2->C3, P3->C1, P4->C2, P5->C3
5. Each consumer receives its new assignment
6. Consumers resume from last committed offsets
```

## Scaling Strategies

- **More partitions** = more parallelism (but more file handles, more rebalancing overhead)
- **More brokers** = more storage and throughput capacity
- **Tiered storage**: move old segments to S3/GCS for cheap long-term retention
- **Compression**: producer-side compression (gzip, lz4, zstd) reduces network and storage by 60-80%
- **Batching**: producers accumulate messages and send in batches to reduce network round trips

## Trade-offs

| Decision | Pros | Cons |
|----------|------|------|
| Append-only log | Incredible write throughput, simple | Compaction needed for key-based topics |
| Partition-level ordering | Scales horizontally | No global ordering across partitions |
| Pull-based consumers | Consumer controls pace, supports replay | Requires polling, slightly higher latency |
| Replication | Durability, fault tolerance | 3x storage, replication lag |
| Consumer group rebalancing | Automatic scaling | Stop-the-world pauses during rebalance |
| At-least-once default | Simpler, higher throughput | Consumers must be idempotent |

## Failure Handling

- **Broker failure**: Leader partition fails over to an ISR (in-sync replica). Unclean leader election can cause data loss.
- **Consumer failure**: Session timeout triggers rebalance; partitions reassigned to remaining consumers.
- **Network partition**: Split-brain risk. Use `min.insync.replicas=2` with `acks=all` to prevent writes when quorum is lost.
- **Disk failure**: Replicas on other brokers take over. Replace disk and re-replicate.

## Summary

A distributed message queue like Kafka uses append-only commit logs for extreme write throughput, partitioning for horizontal scale, replication for durability, and consumer groups for parallel processing. The key design insight is treating the log as the fundamental storage abstraction — everything else (replay, retention, consumer coordination) follows naturally from this choice.
9.6.1.intuition: Think of a message queue as a massive, distributed notebook with numbered pages. Writers add pages at the end (append-only), readers bookmark where they stopped (offsets), and multiple reading groups can independently track their own progress through the same notebook without interfering with each other.

exercises.title: Build a Single-Partition Message Queue
exercises.description: Implement a simple message queue in Node.js using an append-only file as the commit log. Support produce (append message), consume (read from offset), and commit offset operations. Use fs.appendFile for writes and fs.createReadStream for reads.
exercises.hint: Store each message as a line of JSON. Track offsets as byte positions in the file. Use readline to parse messages from a given byte offset.

exercises.title: Implement Consumer Groups
exercises.description: Extend your message queue to support consumer groups. Create a topic with 4 partitions and 2 consumers in a group. Messages with the same key should always go to the same partition. When a consumer disconnects, its partitions should be redistributed.
exercises.hint: Use a hash function on the message key to determine the partition. Store consumer-partition assignments in a Map. Implement a simple round-robin rebalance when membership changes.

exercises.title: Benchmark Batching vs Single Writes
exercises.description: Write a producer that sends 100,000 messages. Compare throughput and latency when sending one message at a time versus batching 500 messages per write. Measure messages/second and p99 latency for both approaches.
exercises.hint: Use process.hrtime.bigint() for precise timing. Accumulate messages in an array and flush when the batch is full or a timeout (e.g., 5ms) expires.

exercises.title: Design a Dead Letter Queue
exercises.description: Implement a DLQ mechanism: if a consumer fails to process a message after 3 retries, move it to a special dead-letter topic. Include the original topic, partition, offset, error message, and retry count in the DLQ message.
exercises.hint: Wrap your consumer processing in a retry loop with exponential backoff. After max retries, publish to a `{topic}-dlq` topic with enriched metadata.

problems.problem: Consumer group rebalancing causes processing delays and duplicate messages
problems.cause: When a consumer joins or leaves, all consumers in the group must pause, revoke their partitions, and receive new assignments. During this window, no messages are processed, and uncommitted messages will be re-delivered.
problems.solution: Use cooperative incremental rebalancing (KIP-429) instead of eager rebalancing. Configure longer session timeouts to avoid unnecessary rebalances from transient issues. Use static group membership where consumers have a persistent identity.
problems.symptoms: Periodic latency spikes in consumer metrics. Duplicate processing of messages after rebalances. Consumer lag spikes visible in monitoring.

problems.problem: Producer throughput degrades as the number of partitions increases
problems.cause: Each partition requires a separate network connection and in-flight request buffer. The producer's linger.ms and batch.size settings may not be tuned for the partition count, causing many small requests.
problems.solution: Increase linger.ms to allow larger batches. Use compression to reduce network overhead. Limit the number of partitions per topic to what is actually needed for consumer parallelism. Use a connection pool with max connections per broker.
problems.symptoms: Network throughput is low despite high CPU usage on the producer. Many small TCP packets visible in network monitoring.

problems.problem: Messages are delivered out of order within a partition
problems.cause: When retries are enabled and max.in.flight.requests.per.connection > 1, a failed batch can be retried after a subsequent batch succeeds, causing reordering.
problems.solution: Set max.in.flight.requests.per.connection to 1 for strict ordering (sacrifices throughput). Alternatively, enable idempotent producer mode which handles ordering automatically with sequence numbers.

further_reading.title: The Log: What every software engineer should know about real-time data's unifying abstraction
further_reading.note: Jay Kreps' foundational article that inspired Kafka's design

further_reading.title: Kafka: The Definitive Guide (2nd Edition)
further_reading.note: Comprehensive guide covering architecture, operations, and streaming

further_reading.title: KafkaJS Documentation
further_reading.note: The standard Node.js client for Apache Kafka

further_reading.title: Designing Data-Intensive Applications - Chapter 11: Stream Processing
further_reading.note: Martin Kleppmann's deep dive into stream processing and message brokers

9.6.2.body:
# Design an Email Service (Gmail)

## Introduction

Email is one of the oldest and most widely used distributed systems on the internet. Designing a large-scale email service like Gmail involves handling billions of messages daily, supporting full-text search across massive mailboxes, managing attachments efficiently, and maintaining real-time notifications. This is a rich system design problem that touches storage, search, queueing, and push notifications.

## Requirements

### Functional Requirements

- Users can **send** emails to one or many recipients (including CC, BCC)
- Users can **receive** emails and see them in their inbox
- Support **folders** (Inbox, Sent, Drafts, Trash, Spam, custom labels)
- Full-text **search** across all emails
- Support **attachments** up to 25 MB per email
- **Push notifications** for new email arrival
- Support **threading** — group related emails into conversations
- Mark emails as read/unread, star, archive
- Spam and phishing **detection**

### Non-Functional Requirements

- 1 billion active users
- 300 billion emails sent/received per day (across all users)
- Average mailbox: 10 GB (growing)
- Search latency: < 500 ms for full-text search
- Availability: 99.99%
- Durability: zero data loss for stored email

## Capacity Estimation

```
Users:                  1 billion
Emails/day:             300 billion (~3,500 per second per user is wrong; 
                        avg ~100 emails/user/day, many are B2B/automated)
Emails/second:          300B / 86,400 = ~3.5 million emails/sec
Avg email size:         50 KB (without attachments)
With attachments (20%): 50 KB * 0.8 + 500 KB * 0.2 = 140 KB avg
Storage/day:            3.5M/s * 140 KB * 86,400 = ~42 PB/day
Storage/year:           ~15 EB (exabytes)
Bandwidth (ingress):    3.5M/s * 140 KB = ~490 GB/s

Simplified per-user:
  Emails/day:           100
  Storage growth/day:   100 * 140 KB = 14 MB/day
  Storage growth/year:  ~5 GB/year
```

## High-Level Architecture

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        Clients                               │
│  ┌────────┐  ┌────────┐  ┌────────┐  ┌─────────────────┐   │
│  │Web App │  │Mobile  │  │Desktop │  │SMTP/IMAP Clients│   │
```

## API Design

### Send Email

```javascript
import express from 'express';
import multer from 'multer';
const app = express();
const upload = multer({ limits: { fileSize: 25 * 1024 * 1024 } });

// POST /api/v1/emails/send
app.post('/api/v1/emails/send', upload.array('attachments', 10), async (req, res) => {
  const { to, cc, bcc, subject, body, bodyHtml, inReplyTo, threadId } = req.body;
  const attachments = req.files || [];

  // Validate recipients
  const allRecipients = [
    ...parseRecipients(to),
    ...parseRecipients(cc),
    ...parseRecipients(bcc),
  ];
  if (allRecipients.length === 0) {
    return res.status(400).json({ error: 'At least one recipient required' });
  }
  if (allRecipients.length > 500) {
    return res.status(400).json({ error: 'Maximum 500 recipients' });
  }

  // Upload attachments to object store
  const attachmentRefs = await Promise.all(
    attachments.map(file => attachmentStore.upload({
      buffer: file.buffer,
      filename: file.originalname,
      contentType: file.mimetype,
      size: file.size,
    }))
  );

  // Create email record
  const email = await emailService.create({
    from: req.user.email,
    to: parseRecipients(to),
    cc: parseRecipients(cc),
    bcc: parseRecipients(bcc),
    subject,
    body,
    bodyHtml,
    attachments: attachmentRefs,
    threadId: threadId || generateThreadId(),
    inReplyTo,
    messageId: generateMessageId(req.user.domain),
  });

  // Enqueue for async delivery
  await messageQueue.produce('email-send', {
    key: email.id,
    value: email,
  });

  res.status(202).json({
    id: email.id,
    messageId: email.messageId,
    status: 'queued',
  });
});
```

### Read Emails (Inbox)

```javascript
// GET /api/v1/emails?folder=inbox&page=1&limit=50
app.get('/api/v1/emails', async (req, res) => {
  const { folder, page, limit, q } = req.query;
  const userId = req.user.id;

  let emails;
  if (q) {
    // Full-text search via Elasticsearch
    emails = await searchService.searchEmails(userId, {
      query: q,
      folder,
      from: parseInt(page - 1) * parseInt(limit),
      size: parseInt(limit) || 50,
    });
  } else {
    emails = await emailService.listByFolder(userId, {
      folder: folder || 'inbox',
      page: parseInt(page) || 1,
      limit: parseInt(limit) || 50,
    });
  }

  res.json({
    emails: emails.items.map(e => ({
      id: e.id,
      threadId: e.threadId,
      from: e.from,
      to: e.to,
      subject: e.subject,
      snippet: e.body.substring(0, 200),
      isRead: e.isRead,
      isStarred: e.isStarred,
      labels: e.labels,
      hasAttachments: e.attachments.length > 0,
      receivedAt: e.receivedAt,
    })),
    pagination: {
      page: emails.page,
      limit: emails.limit,
      total: emails.total,
      hasMore: emails.hasMore,
    },
  });
});
```

### Thread View

```javascript
// GET /api/v1/emails/threads/:threadId
app.get('/api/v1/emails/threads/:threadId', async (req, res) => {
  const { threadId } = req.params;
  const userId = req.user.id;

  const thread = await emailService.getThread(userId, threadId);
  if (!thread) return res.status(404).json({ error: 'Thread not found' });

  // Mark all unread messages in thread as read
  await emailService.markThreadRead(userId, threadId);

  res.json({
    threadId,
    subject: thread.subject,
    participants: thread.participants,
    messageCount: thread.messages.length,
    messages: thread.messages.map(m => ({
      id: m.id,
      from: m.from,
      to: m.to,
      cc: m.cc,
      body: m.body,
      bodyHtml: m.bodyHtml,
      attachments: m.attachments.map(a => ({
        id: a.id,
        filename: a.filename,
        size: a.size,
        contentType: a.contentType,
        downloadUrl: a.downloadUrl,
      })),
      sentAt: m.sentAt,
    })),
  });
});
```

## Database Schema

```sql
-- User accounts
CREATE TABLE users (
  id            UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
  email         VARCHAR(255) UNIQUE NOT NULL,
  display_name  VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
  password_hash VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
  storage_used  BIGINT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  storage_limit BIGINT NOT NULL DEFAULT 15737418240, -- 15 GB
  created_at    TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW()
);

-- Emails (denormalized for read performance)
-- In production this would be a wide-column store like Bigtable or Cassandra
CREATE TABLE emails (
  id            UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
  message_id    VARCHAR(512) UNIQUE NOT NULL,  -- RFC 5322 Message-ID
  thread_id     VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
  from_addr     VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
  to_addrs      TEXT[] NOT NULL,
  cc_addrs      TEXT[] DEFAULT '{}',
  bcc_addrs     TEXT[] DEFAULT '{}',
  subject       TEXT NOT NULL,
  body_text     TEXT,
  body_html     TEXT,
  size_bytes    INT NOT NULL,
  in_reply_to   VARCHAR(512),
  has_attachments BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT FALSE,
  spam_score    FLOAT DEFAULT 0.0,
  created_at    TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW()
);

CREATE INDEX idx_emails_thread ON emails(thread_id, created_at);
CREATE INDEX idx_emails_message_id ON emails(message_id);

-- User-email relationship (per-user view of emails)
CREATE TABLE user_emails (
  id            UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
  user_id       UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id),
  email_id      UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES emails(id),
  folder        VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL DEFAULT 'inbox',
  labels        TEXT[] DEFAULT '{}',
  is_read       BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT FALSE,
  is_starred    BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT FALSE,
  is_draft      BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT FALSE,
  is_spam       BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT FALSE,
  is_trash      BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT FALSE,
  snoozed_until TIMESTAMPTZ,
  received_at   TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
  UNIQUE (user_id, email_id)
);

CREATE INDEX idx_user_emails_folder ON user_emails(user_id, folder, received_at DESC);
CREATE INDEX idx_user_emails_labels ON user_emails USING GIN(labels);
CREATE INDEX idx_user_emails_unread ON user_emails(user_id, folder)
  WHERE is_read = FALSE;

-- Attachments metadata
CREATE TABLE attachments (
  id            UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
  email_id      UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES emails(id),
  filename      VARCHAR(512) NOT NULL,
  content_type  VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
  size_bytes    INT NOT NULL,
  storage_key   VARCHAR(1024) NOT NULL, -- S3/GCS key
  checksum      VARCHAR(64) NOT NULL,   -- SHA-256
  created_at    TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW()
);

CREATE INDEX idx_attachments_email ON attachments(email_id);

-- Labels (user-defined)
CREATE TABLE labels (
  id            UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
  user_id       UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id),
  name          VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
  color         VARCHAR(7),
  created_at    TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
  UNIQUE (user_id, name)
);
```

## Deep Dive: Email Delivery Pipeline

```
Sender composes email
        │
        ▼
┌─────────────────┐
│ Email Send Svc  │──> Store in Sent folder for sender
└────────┬────────┘
         │
         ▼
┌─────────────────┐    ┌──────────────────┐
│ Spam/Virus      │───>│ Rejected? Bounce  │
│ Filter          │    │ back to sender    │
└────────┬────────┘    └──────────────────┘
         │ (passes)
         ▼
┌─────────────────┐
│ Routing Logic   │
│                 │
│ Internal user?  │──Yes──> Direct delivery to mailbox
│                 │
│ External user?  │──Yes──> SMTP outbound delivery
└────────┬────────┘
         │
         ▼
┌─────────────────┐
│ Mailbox Write   │──> Write to email store
│                 │──> Update search index
│                 │──> Send push notification
│                 │──> Update unread count cache
└─────────────────┘
```

## Scaling Strategies

- **Sharding by user ID**: Each user's mailbox lives on a deterministic shard. This is the primary scaling axis.
- **Attachment deduplication**: Hash attachments; if the same file is sent to 1000 people, store it once.
- **Search index partitioning**: Each user's search index is colocated with their email shard.
- **Read replicas**: Heavy read workload (inbox refresh) served by replicas; writes go to the leader.
- **CDN for attachments**: Large files served from edge locations.
- **Tiered storage**: Recent emails on SSD, older emails on cheaper HDD/object storage.

## Trade-offs

| Decision | Pros | Cons |
|----------|------|------|
| Shard by user ID | Queries are single-shard | Cross-user queries (admin) require scatter-gather |
| Separate attachment store (S3) | Dedup, CDN, cheap storage | Extra hop to fetch, eventual consistency |
| Async delivery via queue | Absorbs spikes, retry logic | Email not instant — slight delay |
| Elasticsearch for search | Powerful full-text search | Another system to maintain, index lag |
| Denormalized user_emails table | Fast reads per user | Data duplication across recipients |

## Summary

Designing an email service at scale requires separating the write path (sending/receiving) from the read path (inbox/search), using message queues for reliable async processing, object storage for attachments, full-text search engines for queries, and sharding by user for horizontal scale. The key insight is that email is fundamentally a per-user system — almost every query is scoped to a single user, making user-based sharding natural and effective.
9.6.2.intuition: Think of an email service like a postal system for a city of 1 billion people. Each person has a private mailbox (sharded storage). The post office (SMTP gateway) receives mail, a sorting facility (spam filter + routing) processes it, and letter carriers (delivery workers) place it in the right mailbox. The key insight: you organize everything around individual mailboxes, not around letters.

exercises.title: Build an Email Send Pipeline
exercises.description: Create a Node.js service with Express that accepts an email send request, validates it, stores the email in a database (SQLite or PostgreSQL), and enqueues it for delivery using BullMQ. Create a worker that processes the queue, simulates SMTP delivery, and updates the email status.
exercises.hint: Use BullMQ with Redis for the queue. Create separate 'send' and 'delivery-status' queues. The worker should simulate delivery with a random delay and occasionally fail to test retry logic.

exercises.title: Implement Email Threading
exercises.description: Build a threading system that groups emails into conversations. Use the In-Reply-To and References headers to link emails. Given a set of 20 emails, correctly group them into threads and display them in chronological order within each thread.
exercises.hint: Parse the Message-ID, In-Reply-To, and References headers. Build a tree structure where each email is a node. Use the References header as a fallback when In-Reply-To is missing.

exercises.title: Full-Text Search with Elasticsearch
exercises.description: Set up Elasticsearch and index 1000 sample emails. Implement search that supports: keyword search in subject and body, filtering by date range, filtering by sender, and highlighting matched terms in results.
exercises.hint: Use the @elastic/elasticsearch Node.js client. Create a mapping with text fields for subject and body, keyword fields for from/to, and date fields. Use multi_match queries with highlighting.

problems.problem: Inbox loading is slow for users with 100,000+ emails
problems.cause: The inbox query scans too many rows even with pagination because the index on (user_id, folder, received_at) is not selective enough, or the query plan does a sort on disk for non-indexed label combinations.
problems.solution: Ensure a covering index on (user_id, folder, received_at DESC) that includes the columns needed for the inbox list view. Consider materialized views for common folder views. Use cursor-based pagination (WHERE received_at < :lastSeen) instead of OFFSET-based pagination.
problems.symptoms: Inbox API response time > 2 seconds for power users. Database CPU spikes during inbox loads.

problems.problem: Search index falls behind during high email volume periods
problems.cause: The search indexer cannot keep up with the email ingestion rate. Elasticsearch bulk indexing is bottlenecked by JVM garbage collection or disk I/O.
problems.solution: Increase the search indexer consumer parallelism. Use Elasticsearch bulk API with optimal batch sizes (5-15 MB per batch). Implement backpressure — if the indexing queue grows too large, temporarily buffer in Kafka and replay. Consider separate hot/warm Elasticsearch clusters.
problems.symptoms: Users search for a recently received email and cannot find it. Search index consumer lag grows during peak hours.

problems.problem: Attachment storage costs are growing faster than user base
problems.cause: The same attachment (e.g., a company-wide PDF) is stored once per recipient instead of being deduplicated. Deleted emails still have their attachments stored.
problems.solution: Implement content-addressable storage: hash each attachment and store it once. Use reference counting to track how many emails reference each attachment. Run a garbage collector that deletes unreferenced attachments after a grace period.

9.6.3.intuition: Think of a logging pipeline as a river system. Thousands of tributaries (services) feed into a main river (Kafka). The river flows through treatment plants (processors) that filter and enrich the water. Some water goes to a fast-access reservoir (Elasticsearch for real-time queries), some to a large lake (ClickHouse for analytics), and some to long-term underground storage (S3 for archival). Sensors along the way trigger alarms (alerts) when pollution levels spike.

exercises.title: Build a Log Ingestion Service
exercises.description: Create a Node.js Express service that accepts JSON log entries via HTTP POST, validates the schema, enriches them with a server timestamp and request metadata, and writes them to a local file in JSONL format (one JSON object per line). Implement batching — accumulate up to 100 logs or 1 second before flushing to disk.
exercises.hint: Use a buffer array and setInterval for time-based flushing. Use fs.appendFile in write mode. Implement a flush function that JSON-stringifies each log on a separate line and writes the batch atomically.

exercises.title: Implement a Real-Time Log Tail
exercises.description: Build a WebSocket endpoint that streams new log entries to connected clients in real time. Clients should be able to subscribe with filters (e.g., service=UserService AND level=ERROR). Only matching logs should be sent to each client.
exercises.hint: Use the 'ws' npm package. Store connected clients with their filter criteria. When a new log is ingested, evaluate it against all client filters and send to matching clients. Parse filter expressions into a function.

exercises.title: Build a Simple Alert Engine
exercises.description: Create an alerting system that evaluates rules against a sliding window of logs. Support rules like 'if ERROR count > 10 in 5 minutes, alert'. Implement cooldown so the same alert does not fire repeatedly. Send alerts to a console output or a webhook.
exercises.hint: Use a circular buffer or array of timestamped events per rule. Every 30 seconds, evaluate each rule against the window. Track last alert time per rule for cooldown. Use node-fetch to call webhooks.

problems.problem: Log ingestion service becomes a bottleneck during traffic spikes, causing log loss
problems.cause: The ingestion service writes synchronously to downstream systems (Elasticsearch directly) and cannot absorb sudden traffic increases. Backpressure from slow consumers causes the ingestion API to timeout.
problems.solution: Always put a message queue (Kafka) between ingestion and storage. The ingestion service should only validate and enqueue — never write directly to the storage layer. Implement local disk buffering in agents as a fallback. Use acks=1 on Kafka for speed.
problems.symptoms: HTTP 503 errors from the ingestion API during traffic spikes. Missing log entries for peak traffic periods. Agent retry queues growing.

problems.problem: Elasticsearch cluster runs out of disk space and becomes read-only
problems.cause: Index lifecycle management is not configured. Old indices are not deleted or moved to cheaper storage. The cluster hits the low-watermark (85% disk) and stops accepting writes.
problems.solution: Implement ILM policies that rollover indices daily, force-merge after 1 day, move to warm after 7 days, delete after 30 days. Monitor disk usage with alerts at 70% and 80%. Use curator or ILM to automate index management.
problems.symptoms: Elasticsearch returns 403 Forbidden with 'cluster_block_exception' on write requests. No new logs appear in Kibana.

problems.problem: Dashboard queries for 30-day aggregations take over 60 seconds
problems.cause: Elasticsearch is querying across hundreds of daily indices with billions of documents. Aggregation queries require scanning all matching documents.
problems.solution: Pre-compute rollup aggregations (hourly/daily summaries) and store them in a dedicated analytics store (ClickHouse or TimescaleDB). Dashboard queries hit the rollup tables instead of raw data. For common queries, cache results in Redis with a 5-minute TTL.

9.6.4.body:
# Design a Recommendation Engine

## Introduction

Recommendation engines power the core experience of platforms like YouTube, Netflix, Spotify, Amazon, and TikTok. They analyze user behavior, item characteristics, and interaction patterns to suggest content that users are most likely to engage with. Designing a recommendation system at scale involves real-time data pipelines, machine learning model serving, feature stores, A/B testing infrastructure, and careful handling of cold-start problems.

## Requirements

### Functional Requirements

- Generate personalized recommendations for each user ("Recommended for You")
- Support multiple recommendation types: home feed, "similar items", "users also liked", "trending"
- Update recommendations in near real-time as users interact with content
- Support **content-based filtering** (recommend items similar to what user liked)
- Support **collaborative filtering** (recommend items that similar users liked)
- Handle the **cold-start problem** for new users and new items
- Support A/B testing of different recommendation algorithms
- Provide explainability ("Because you watched X")

### Non-Functional Requirements

- 500 million daily active users
- Recommendation API latency: p99 < 100 ms
- Generate candidate pool of 10,000 items, rank to top 100, serve top 20
- Update user profile with new interactions within 5 minutes
- Support 100,000 recommendation requests/second

## Capacity Estimation

```
DAU:                     500 million
Recommendation requests: 500M * 10 sessions * 5 pages = 25 billion/day
Requests/second:         25B / 86,400 = ~290,000 req/s (peak 2-3x)
Peak requests/second:    ~600,000-900,000 req/s

User profiles:           500 million * 5 KB avg = ~2.5 TB
Item catalog:            50 million items * 2 KB = ~100 GB
Interaction events/day:  500M * 200 interactions = 100 billion events/day
Interaction storage/day: 100B * 100 bytes = ~10 TB/day

Model serving:
  Candidate generation:  ~5 ms per request
  Ranking model:         ~20 ms per request (batch of 1000)
  Feature lookup:        ~5 ms per request
  Total pipeline:        ~50 ms budget
```
